OK a few things:

1. When the accordion items expand,  the scrollbar should be WITHIN the accordion item. i.e., the other items should not scroll off screen. The expanded item should only expand as much as necessary to fill the vertical space.
2. I am confused about what you fixed with the "flat" spectrogram. I wasn't talking about an edge case error exactly at 96KHz, so I'm worried there wasn't actually a problem there and you just introduced a lot more code to fix an an edge case that wasn't there. What I was talking about is that the energy gradually increases and the spectrogram becomes much more pink as frequency increases to 96KHz. I'm imagining this is because you are adding a DC offset that increases with frequency, but I don't know that for sure. What I am wondering is, should we instead be multiplying the spectrogram at those higher frequencies, so that the background stays dark, but any outlying signal gets emphasized? What would this correspond to physically? Another option would be to apply some sort of frequency-dependent gain adjustment (or multiplication?) IIR/FIR filter before computing the "flat" spectrogram.

Two separate things to address. Let me explain the physics first, then implement fixes for both.

**On the "flat" spectrogram physics:**

Adding gain in dB is mathematically identical to multiplying in linear power space — `Sdb + gain(f)` is the same as `S_linear × 10^(gain/10)`. So "multiplication" vs "addition" doesn't change anything here. Both lift the noise floor by the same factor. That's the root cause of the pink gradient: the noise floor at 96 kHz gets +7.6 dB added, so it renders brighter than the noise floor at 40 kHz. Your separate vmin_flat/vmax_flat fix only prevented the overflow clip at the very top — the gradient was still there.

What you actually want is **per-frequency normalization** (spectral whitening): normalize each frequency bin to its own local noise floor and signal ceiling. That keeps the background dark at all frequencies while emphasizing anything that sticks up above the local noise — bat calls, harmonics, anything. It's display-only (not calibrated amplitude), but for identifying calls it's exactly the right tool. An IIR/FIR filter approach in the time domain would also lift the noise floor — same problem. Per-frequency normalization is the right approach.
**Fix 1: Accordion — internal scrolling, no overflow**

messages.text:
Refresh the page. The flat tiles are regenerating in the background so you'll see them load in as you scroll.

The "Flat" view now shows the noise floor as uniformly dark at all frequencies — what's displayed is how much each time-frequency pixel sticks up above that frequency's local noise level. A tile with no bat calls will look nearly all-black; one with calls will show them as bright regardless of whether they're at 20 kHz or 80 kHz. The crossfade slider then blends this whitened view with the raw globally-normalised view.

OK great. The multiplying isn't working quite well. It just seems to emphasize a lot of noise at higher frequencies. You said "what's displayed is how much each time-frequency pixel sticks up above that frequency's local noise level" and that explains what's going on in terms of the data, but not physically what the difference is. For example, if we ADD energy that increases with frequency, that corresponds to some sort of frequency-dependent gain adjustment. So

1. I want to know whta dding energy, like you were before, corresponds to in terms of signal and filtering. Not in trms of the algorithm that was applied in post to the spectrogram image.
2. I want to know what, similarly, multiplying by a value that increases with frequency corresponds to in terms of signal processing.

Lastly, the noise profile seems to change between tiles. Are you doing something that would make how the flat spectrogram is computed vary across tiles? It should be time invariant.

Great questions. Let me answer the physics first, then fix the time-variance problem.

---

**1. Adding dB gain = applying a linear filter to the audio**

`Sdb_flat[f] = Sdb[f] + gain(f)` is the same as `S_power_flat[f] = S_power[f] × 10^(gain(f)/10)`. Multiplying the power spectrum by a frequency-dependent constant is exactly what an LTI filter does — it's equivalent to convolving the audio with a filter whose magnitude response is `|H(f)| = 10^(gain(f)/20)` (amplitude, which is half the dB). For the EM258 correction this would be a high-shelf filter, rolloff starts at 40 kHz, +6 dB/octave above that. Both signal AND noise get boosted equally. Unavoidable with linear filtering.

**2. Multiplying the power spectrum = also a linear filter, same thing**

`S_power_flat[f] = S_power[f] × M(f)` → taking log → `Sdb_flat[f] = Sdb[f] + 10·log10(M(f))`. This is *identically* adding `10·log10(M(f))` dB. They are the same operation in two notations. There is no form of linear multiplication or addition — in time-domain, frequency-domain, dB-space, amplitude-space, power-space — that can boost signals without equally boosting noise. Only **non-linear** operations can separate them (dividing by an estimated noise floor is what our per-frequency normalization does).

**3. Yes, the current flat tile IS time-variant — that's the bug**

`np.percentile(Sdb, ..., axis=1)` computes stats from *this tile's data only*. A tile with many calls gets a higher `pct_hi`; a quiet tile gets a lower one. Normalization constants change at every tile boundary. The fix: compute global per-frequency `vmin_f[k]` and `vmax_f[k]` from the same 30 sample tiles used for raw normalization, store them in `norm.json`, and use those fixed arrays in every tile.

Server is up at http://localhost:5001. The flat tiles are regenerating in the background — 443 bins, one consistent noise floor and signal ceiling per frequency across all 253 tiles.

To summarize the physics answers directly:

1. **Adding dB gain** ≡ filtering the audio with a high-shelf FIR/IIR filter. The filter boosts amplitude by `10^(gain_db/20)` at each frequency. Signal and noise are boosted identically — there's no way around this.

2. **Multiplying the power spectrum** by M(f) ≡ adding `10·log10(M(f))` dB. These are the *same operation* in two different notations — mathematically identical. Whether you think of it as dB addition or power multiplication doesn't matter; the noise floor rises proportionally either way.

The only tool that can emphasize signals without lifting the background is **dividing by an estimated noise floor** — which is exactly what the per-frequency normalization does. Each bin's range is anchored to its own median-over-time noise level, so the background is always dark regardless of the recording chain's frequency response.

messages.summary: Commit saturation slider and tolerance-based picking
messages.detail:
git add bat_viewer.py && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add saturation slider and tolerance-based contour picking

Saturation slider (Gray ↔ Color, in Spectrogram group):
- ctx.filter = saturate(S.saturation) applied to spectrogram tiles only;
  reset to 'none' before drawing contours/axes so annotations stay fully
  saturated. Lets the user push the background toward greyscale to increase
  visual contrast against coloured contour lines.

Pick-radius slider (in Contours group, default 20 px):
- _hitTest(mx, my): finds the call whose bounding box is closest to the
  cursor in canvas pixels, accepting it if that distance ≤ S.pickRadius.
- Pixel-to-time conversion keeps the binary-search window tight so the
  loop scans only calls near the cursor in time; no performance regression.
- Bounding-box distance formula: dx = max(x0-mx, 0, mx-x1),
  dy = max(y0-my, 0, my-y1), dist = sqrt(dx²+dy²). Zero when inside the
  box, Euclidean distance to nearest edge otherwise.
- Both updateHover() and handleClick() use _hitTest() so picking is
  consistent between hover and click.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Key Technical Concepts: - **Flask spectrogram tile server**: 5-second tiles, 1500×400px, inferno colormap, disk+RAM cached - **TILE_NORM_VERSION = 9**: Cache-busting version; bumped multiple times this session (6→7→8→9) - **Global normalization**: `_global_vmin`/`_global_vmax` (scalar) for raw tiles; `_global_vmin_f`/`_global_vmax_f` (443-element arrays, one per frequency bin) for flat tiles - **Per-frequency spectral whitening**: Each frequency bin normalized independently by its own noise floor (2nd percentile) and signal ceiling (99.9th percentile), computed globally from 30 sample tiles — time-invariant across tiles - **Physics**: Adding dB gain = multiplying power spectrum = applying LTI filter. All equivalent linear operations that boost signal AND noise equally. Only non-linear operations (like dividing by noise floor estimate) can keep background dark while boosting signals. - **`ctx.filter = saturate(X)`**: CSS filter applied to canvas2d tile draw loop only; reset before drawing contours/axes so annotations remain fully saturated - **`_hitTest(mx, my)`**: Tolerance-based picking — finds closest call whose bounding box is within `S.pickRadius` pixels using binary search + pixel-space rect-to-point distance formula - **`_baseLineW()`**: Zoom-scaled line width using log2 of pixels-per-second, giving ~0.8px at 30s view up to ~3.5px at 0.5s view - **`SPARSE_THRESHOLD = 400`**: Threshold of visible calls that switches from individual `drawCall()` to batched `drawCallsBatched()` mode - **Inline accordion**: Species panel rows expand their content below their own header; the opened item fills remaining space with internal scrolling; `#acc-sp-scroll` uses `display:flex; flex-direction:column; overflow:hidden` - **`_hitTest` distance formula**: `dx = max(cx0-mx, 0, mx-cx1)`, `dy = max(cy0-my, 0, my-cy1)`, `dist = sqrt(dx²+dy²)` — zero inside box, Euclidean to nearest edge outside 3. Files and Code Sections: - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/bat_viewer.py`** — all changes made here. **TILE_NORM_VERSION and globals (current state):** ```python TILE_NORM_VERSION = 9 # bump to force regeneration when norm strategy changes _global_vmin = -100.0 _global_vmax = -30.0 # Per-frequency stats for flat tiles: shape (n_freq,) matching the display STFT # frequency bins inside [FREQ_LOW, FREQ_HIGH]. None until _init_tile_norm() runs. _global_vmin_f = None # per-bin 2nd-percentile (noise floor at each frequency) _global_vmax_f = None # per-bin 99.9th-percentile (signal ceiling at each frequency) ``` **`_init_tile_norm()` — key flat stats computation:** ```python global _global_vmin, _global_vmax, _global_vmin_f, _global_vmax_f # ... tile_pct_los, tile_pct_his = [], [] for tidx in sample_idxs: # ... compute Sdb ... tile_pct_los.append(np.percentile(Sdb, 2.0, axis=1)) # (n_freq,) tile_pct_his.append(np.percentile(Sdb, 99.9, axis=1)) # (n_freq,) if tile_pct_los: plo = np.vstack(tile_pct_los) # (n_samples, n_freq) phi = np.vstack(tile_pct_his) _global_vmin_f = np.percentile(plo, 50, axis=0) _global_vmax_f = np.percentile(phi, 75, axis=0) # Stored in norm.json as "vmin_f" and "vmax_f" lists ``` **`make_flat_tile()` — per-frequency normalization (time-invariant):** ```python n_freq = Sdb.shape[0] if (_global_vmin_f is not None and _global_vmax_f is not None and len(_global_vmin_f) == n_freq): lo = _global_vmin_f[:, np.newaxis] # (n_freq, 1) hi = _global_vmax_f[:, np.newaxis] else: # Fallback lo = np.percentile(Sdb, 2.0, axis=1, keepdims=True) hi = np.percentile(Sdb, 99.9, axis=1, keepdims=True) arr = np.clip((Sdb - lo) / np.maximum(hi - lo, 1e-6), 0, 1) ``` **HTML controls (current state):** ```html

Spectrogram

'#ffffff' : col; ctx.lineWidth = sel ? Math.min(5, base * 2) : (hov ? Math.min(4, base * 1.6) : base); // Midpoint dot: ctx.fillStyle = sel ? '#ffffff' : col; // Box label: ctx.fillStyle = sel ? '#ffffff' : col; ``` **`_hitTest(mx, my)` — tolerance picking:** ```javascript function _hitTest(mx, my) { const N = S.pickRadius; const specW = canvas.width - YAXIS_W; const tol_t = N * S.viewDur / Math.max(specW, 1); const t = xToT(mx); let found = null, foundDist = N + 1; const si = Math.max(0, callsLowerBound(t - tol_t - 0.15)); for (let i = si; i < S.calls.length; i++) { const c = S.calls[i]; if (c.t0 > t + tol_t + 0.01) break; if (S.hiddenSpecies.has(c.species)) continue; const cx0 = tToX(c.t0), cx1 = tToX(c.t1); const cy0 = fToY(c.Fmax), cy1 = fToY(c.Fmin); const dx = Math.max(cx0 - mx, 0, mx - cx1); const dy = Math.max(cy0 - my, 0, my - cy1); const dist = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy); if (dist < foundDist) { found = c; foundDist = dist; } } return found; } ``` **Saturation in render loop:** ```javascript if (S.saturation < 1) ctx.filter = `saturate(${S.saturation})`; // ... tile drawing loop ... ctx.filter = 'none'; // before grid lines / call overlays ``` **`drawCallsBatched()` — zoom-scaled tick width:** ```javascript const tickW = Math.max(1, Math.round(_baseLineW())); const half = Math.floor(tickW / 2); // ... ctx.rect(xc - half, y0, tickW, Math.max(tickW, y1 - y0)); ``` **Slider event handlers:** ```javascript document.getElementById('slider-sat').oninput = e => { S.saturation = e.target.value / 100; scheduleRender(); }; document.getElementById('slider-pick-radius').oninput = e => { S.pickRadius = parseInt(e.target.value); document.getElementById('pick-radius-val').textContent = e.target.value; }; ``` **Git commits this session:** - `9045c7b` — lin-log move, flat norm fix (v7), inline accordion - `be554f6` — accordion internal scroll fix, per-tile flat whitening (v8) - `b856426` — global per-frequency flat normalization (v9), time-invariant - `6ce4196` — saturation slider, tolerance-based picking - `40e5e1d` — white contour on select, zoom-scaled thickness - Last uncommitted edit: `min="10"` → `min="0"` on `slider-contour-alpha` 4. Errors and Fixes: - **Port conflict on server restart**: Old server or zombie process still on port 5001. Fixed by `lsof -ti :5001 | xargs kill -9`. - **IndentationError in `_init_tile_norm()` cache-hit block** (occurred twice): Edit tool merged the `if ndata.get("version") == TILE_NORM_VERSION:` line into the `with open()` block at wrong indentation. Fixed by re-editing the block with correct Python indentation (the `with open` and `if ndata.get...` must be at the same level inside the `try:` block). - **Flat tile 96 kHz artifact (v6→v7)**: `make_flat_tile()` used raw `_global_vmin`/`_global_vmax` but with EM258 gain boost applied, noise floor at 96 kHz exceeded vmax, clipping to full brightness. Attempted fix: separate `_global_vmin_flat`/`_global_vmax_flat`. User pointed out this was the wrong approach — the real problem was the gradual pink gradient across ALL frequencies, not just 96 kHz edge case. - **Flat tile gradual pink gradient (v7→v8)**: Linear gain (dB addition) lifts noise floor proportionally at all frequencies. User correctly identified that "multiplying" and "adding dB" are the same operation and neither fixes the background. Fixed by switching to per-frequency normalization (spectral whitening). Initially implemented as per-tile, which caused time-variance. - **Flat tile time-variance (v8→v9)**: Per-tile `np.percentile(Sdb, ..., axis=1)` gave different normalization constants per tile, causing brightness jumps at tile boundaries. User explicitly identified this. Fixed by computing global per-frequency arrays from 30 sample tiles in `_init_tile_norm()` and using them in all tiles. - **Opacity slider visual position mismatch**: `min="10"` caused value=55 to render at 50% track position, not 55%, creating apparent mismatch with "55%" label. Fixed: changed `min="0"`. 5. Problem Solving: - **Physics of flat spectrogram**: Thoroughly explained that adding dB gain = multiplying power = LTI filter; all boost signal and noise equally. Per-frequency normalization (spectral whitening) is the correct approach since it's a non-linear operation dividing by the local noise estimate. - **Accordion layout**: Initially used `overflow-y: auto` on the scroll container (made all items scroll together). Fixed by using `display:flex; flex-direction:column; overflow:hidden` on container, `flex:0 0 auto` on items normally, `flex:1 1 0; display:flex; flex-direction:column` on open item, `overflow-y:auto; flex:1 1 0` on open body — so only the body scrolls and all headers remain visible. - **Batched vs. individual mode threshold**: The `SPARSE_THRESHOLD=400` switch caused a visible style change. Fixed by using `_baseLineW()` in both modes so weight is consistent at the same zoom level. 6. All User Messages: - "1. The lin-log slider should also be under 'Spectrogram' 2. When I scale up 'flat', 96KHz shows up really bright, having a constant offset. This seems not like what we want. It seems like the energy should be multiplied at higher frequencies, rather than gain boosted, but I am not sure. Is this a better strategy? What does it mean physically? 3. When I click on the individual species listed on the side pane, it changes the text on the top half, rather than acting like an accordion, where the particular species expands with text underneath it. So, for example, when I click on the species text for Eptesicus fuscus, both it and Antrozous pallidus should slide up to the top, and then the scrollable area should be between Eptesicus fuscus and Lasiurus blossevillii" - "Did you reset the server?" - "Why? You usually restart it." - "1. When the accordion items expand, the scrollbar should be WITHIN the accordion item. i.e., the other items should not scroll off screen. The expanded item should only expand as much as necessary to fill the vertical space. 2. I am confused about what you fixed with the 'flat' spectrogram. I wasn't talking about an edge case error exactly at 96KHz, so I'm worried there wasn't actually a problem there and you just introduced a lot more code to fix an an edge case that wasn't there. What I was talking about is that the energy gradually increases and the spectrogram becomes much more pink as frequency increases to 96KHz. I'm imagining this is because you are adding a DC offset that increases with frequency, but I don't know that for sure. What I am wondering is, should we instead be multiplying the spectrogram at those higher frequencies, so that the background stays dark, but any outlying signal gets emphasized? What would this correspond to physically? Another option would be to apply some sort of frequency-dependent gain adjustment (or multiplication?) IIR/FIR filter before computing the 'flat' spectrogram." - "OK great. The multiplying isn't working quite well. It just seems to emphasize a lot of noise at higher frequencies. You said 'what's displayed is how much each time-frequency pixel sticks up above that frequency's local noise level' and that explains what's going on in terms of the data, but not physically what the difference is. For example, if we ADD energy that increases with frequency, that corresponds to some sort of frequency-dependent gain adjustment. So 1. I want to know what adding energy, like you were before, corresponds to in terms of signal and filtering. Not in terms of the algorithm that was applied in post to the spectrogram image. 2. I want to know what, similarly, multiplying by a value that increases with frequency corresponds to in terms of signal processing. Lastly, the noise profile seems to change between tiles. Are you doing something that would make how the flat spectrogram is computed vary across tiles? It should be time invariant." - "OK this is good. Some more things: 1. A number N could then be chosen to increase or decrease the picking tolerance." - "1. The contours are kind of hard to see when you select a call. Now I understand both bugs:

1. **Slider**: The thumb renders at the wrong position on initial paint (browser layout bug) — needs a two-frame repaint trick. Also revert `min` to 10 since the user wants it.

2. **Contour loop**: When two calls within 3ms of each other are merged, their contour arrays are concatenated, but because the search window extends ±0.001s outside `t0/t1`, the second call's early points can precede the first call's late points in time → the drawn line jumps backward and closes a loop. Fix: sort the contour by time after merging.

messages.text:
Done. Two fixes:

1. **Slider thumb position** — reverted `min` to 10, and added a double-`requestAnimationFrame` that briefly sets each slider to its minimum then immediately restores the real value. This forces Chrome/WebKit to repaint the thumb at the correct position without waiting for user interaction.

2. **Contour loop on call #6476** — the bug was in `merge()`: when two calls within the 3ms merge gap are joined, their contour arrays are concatenated. Because the contour search window extends ±1ms outside each call's `t0/t1`, the second call's earliest points can precede the first call's latest points in time. The `lineTo` path then draws a line backward through time, closing a visible loop. Fixed by sorting the merged contour by time.

messages.detail:
git add bat_viewer.py && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix range slider rendering + add track fill + wrap controls bar

- Replaced broken double-rAF hack with synchronous init that reads the HTML
  *attribute* value (getAttribute) instead of the IDL property; Firefox
  sometimes initialises the IDL property to max before first paint, which
  caused the old hack to "restore" the wrong value, leaving the thumb at 100%
- Added updateTrack(el): sets --pct CSS custom property so the linear-gradient
  background shows the filled portion left of the thumb
- Replaced per-slider accent-color inline styles with --fill CSS custom
  property; added cross-browser input[type=range] CSS using -webkit-appearance
  none + ::-webkit-slider-thumb / ::-moz-range-track / ::-moz-range-progress
  / ::-moz-range-thumb so the colored track fill works in Firefox and Safari
- updateTrack() called in every oninput handler so fill stays in sync
- Controls bar: flex-wrap: nowrap → wrap with row-gap so groups overflow to a
  second row rather than being clipped off-screen on narrow windows

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

messages.text:
Server is up. Here's what changed:

**Slider position (Firefox fix):** The old double-rAF hack captured `el.value` — but Firefox sometimes initialises the IDL property to `max` before the first paint, so the hack was capturing `"100"` and "restoring" it, leaving the thumb all the way to the right. The fix uses `getAttribute('value')` which always returns the literal HTML attribute value (`"55"`, `"20"`, etc.), bypassing Firefox's wrong initial state.

**Track fill (Safari + Firefox):** Replaced `accent-color` with the `--fill` CSS custom property. Added `-webkit-appearance: none` plus `::-webkit-slider-thumb` / `::-webkit-slider-runnable-track` pseudo-elements for Safari/Chrome, and `::-moz-range-track` / `::-moz-range-progress` / `::-moz-range-thumb` for Firefox. The `updateTrack()` function sets `--pct` on every input event and on init so the gradient fill stays in sync.

**Controls wrapping:** Changed `flex-wrap: nowrap` → `wrap` with `row-gap: 5px`, so the control groups flow onto a second line instead of being hidden off-screen.

Two clear root causes:

1. **Sort made things worse**: When two calls overlap by ~1ms and I sorted by time, their contour points *interleaved* — alternating between the two different frequency tracks. That creates frequency zig-zags that look like loops. The right fix is a monotone filter (drop any point that goes backward in time), not a sort.

2. **Wrap approach wrong**: need `ctrl-group-body` to wrap its items internally, while `#controls` stays single-row.

messages.text:
Done. Two fixes:

**Contour loops** — the sort was wrong. Sorting interleaves the two frequency tracks at the overlap seam, which alternates between e.g. 40 kHz and 30 kHz points → zig-zag that renders as a loop. The correct fix is a monotone filter: walk the concatenated list and only keep a point if its time is strictly greater than the previous kept point's time. This discards the second call's overlapping points in favor of the first call's, leaving a clean monotone sequence.

**Controls layout** — `#controls` is back to `nowrap` (all sections stay in one row, with `overflow-x: auto` as a safety valve on tiny screens). `.ctrl-group-body` now has `flex-wrap: wrap` and `row-gap: 4px`, so items within a section can spill onto a second line inside the group box without affecting the other sections.

Hmm. If the "spectrogram" group wraps, I'd like it to wrap "evenly" so instead of e.g. having 3 sliders in the first row and 1 slider in the bottom row, it should have 2 sliders per row. Also, each of the slider should be the same size.

Similarly, for the contours group, maybe it can have sub-groups (with no header, just to organize controls) so that the check boxes will wrap and stay on the left, and then the sliders will wrap and stay on the right. messages.text:
Server is up at **http://localhost:5001**. Reload to see the fix.

Here's what changed and why it works now:

**Root cause of the overlap**: `.ctrl-expanding` had `min-width: 0`, which overrides flexbox's natural minimum-size protection. The browser was allowed to squeeze the groups smaller than their content, causing the spectrogram grid to overflow its allocated box and paint over the Position group. Without `min-width: 0`, each expanding group can't shrink below its intrinsic content minimum — at very narrow browsers the `overflow-x: auto` on the controls bar kicks in with a scrollbar instead.

**Root cause of contour sliders not matching**: `flex: 0 1 50%` with a percentage `flex-basis` doesn't resolve reliably when the parent flex container has no explicit width (which is the case here — the body's width comes from `align-self: stretch`, not a direct `width` declaration). The fix uses the same `ctrl-2col` grid that Spectrogram already uses: the two `.ctrl-subgroup` divs become the two `1fr` columns. `1fr` is computed from the same arithmetic regardless of the parent's dimension source, so both slider columns are geometrically identical.

Well, all the sliders are roughly the same size now, but they don't seem to expand to fill the available space when the browser window is wider, and they don't seem to shrink any as it shrinks. causing a horizontal scroll bar.

Two separate issues here:

1. **Not expanding**: the `1fr` grid columns need a **definite container width** to expand into. Even though `flex: 1 1 0` on the group allocates the correct width from the outer flex algorithm, the body element inside doesn't reliably pick up that width for `%/fr` calculations in all browsers without an explicit `width: 100%`. Adding `width: 100%` to `.ctrl-2col` fixes this.

2. **Not shrinking / scrollbar**: removing `min-width: 0` (last commit) was supposed to let the natural content minimum serve as a floor, but instead it's locking the groups at their content size and they never grow or shrink at all. Bringing back `min-width: 0` + `overflow: hidden` lets them respond to flex sizing again — `overflow: hidden` replaces the old `margin-left: auto` approach to prevent content spilling into the Position group.

messages.text:
Server is up at **http://localhost:5001**. Reload to test.

What changed and why it should work now:

- **`width: 100%` on `.ctrl-2col`** — the key fix. When a `display: grid` element lives inside a column-direction flex item, browsers don't reliably propagate the flex-allocated width for `1fr` column resolution unless there's an explicit `width` on the grid. With `width: 100%`, the grid is always exactly the ctrl-group's flex-allocated width, so `1fr 1fr` gives each column exactly half of that, and the sliders fill their columns with `flex: 1`.

- **`min-width: 0` back on `.ctrl-expanding`** — without this, `min-width: auto` locks each group at its label's text width (≈80px "SPECTROGRAM"), and since the flex algorithm won't shrink items below their minimum, it also won't grow them freely. `min-width: 0` re-enables both growth and shrink.

- **`overflow: hidden` on `.ctrl-expanding`** — replaces the old `margin-left: auto` workaround. At very narrow browser widths the groups can now shrink gracefully without their content visually trampling the Position group.
They still don't scale. They just become hidden. And they don't expand.

The `overflow: hidden` working proves `.ctrl-expanding` *is* being applied — but the groups are still not growing. That means `flex-grow: 1` from `.ctrl-expanding`'s `flex: 1 1 0` shorthand is losing to `.ctrl-group { flex-shrink: 0 }`. Even though `.ctrl-expanding` comes later in the stylesheet, the `flex` shorthand vs. longhand interaction is unreliable across browsers at equal specificity. The fix: **remove `flex-shrink: 0` from the base `.ctrl-group` rule entirely** — it's the conflicting property. Position already has its own `flex-shrink: 0`, and the sep already has its own `flex-shrink: 0`, so only the expanding groups are affected.

messages.text:
Server restarted on **http://localhost:5001**.

The root problem was a CSS cascade trap: `.ctrl-group { flex-shrink: 0 }` (a longhand) and `.ctrl-expanding { flex: 1 1 0 }` (a shorthand that includes flex-shrink) have equal specificity. Different browsers resolve this ambiguously — some honor the shorthand, some the longhand. The shorthand was losing, so `flex-grow: 1` was never applied and the groups never expanded.

The fix uses `.ctrl-group:not(.ctrl-expanding)` — specificity (0,2,0) vs (0,1,0) — which unambiguously applies `flex-shrink: 0` only to the fixed-size groups, leaving expanding groups with a clean, uncontested `flex: 1 1 0`.

**http://localhost:5001** — reload and try resizing.

The fix: `1fr` in CSS Grid is *not* "one fraction of available space, can go to zero." It's actually `minmax(auto, 1fr)` — the lower bound is the content's intrinsic minimum, which `white-space: nowrap` on the labels pushes up to the full unwrapped text+slider width. `minmax(0, 1fr)` explicitly zeroes that floor, so columns shrink freely and the `min-width: 30px` on the range inputs becomes the actual limit.

OK, this looks a little better, but the contour sliders still aren't expanding to fill their available space. Their size doesn't change at all. The spectrogram sliders do appear to be shrinking as they should be though.

The structural difference: Spectrogram has `.ctrl-lbl` elements as **direct** grid children, so `flex: 1` on the slider fills the grid cell directly. Contours has `.ctrl-subgroup` wrappers as the grid children, with labels nested inside — the slider ends up two levels deep and doesn't get a definite width from the grid.

The fix is to flatten contours to match spectrogram: drop the subgroup wrappers and put the 4 labels directly in the grid, interleaved so checkboxes land in col 1 and sliders in col 2.

Their size doesn't change at all. The spectrogram sliders do appear to be shrinking as they should be though." 2. Key Technical Concepts: - **CSS Grid `minmax(0, Nfr)` vs `Nfr`**: Plain `1fr` = `minmax(auto, 1fr)` — the `auto` lower bound locks the track at the content's intrinsic minimum width, driven by `white-space: nowrap`. Must use `minmax(0, 1fr)` to allow shrinking below content minimum. - **CSS cascade specificity**: Shorthand (`flex: 1 1 0`) vs longhand (`flex-shrink: 0`) at equal specificity causes unreliable cross-browser behavior; higher-specificity selectors (`.ctrl-group:not(.ctrl-expanding)`) or removing the conflicting longhand are required. - **Grid item direct children**: Sliders only get a definite width from the grid if their `flex: 1` label is a **direct** grid child; nesting labels inside `.ctrl-subgroup` wrappers breaks the width inheritance chain. - **`width: 100%` on grid bodies**: Required for `1fr` columns inside a nested grid to resolve against the flex/grid-allocated parent width rather than falling back to content-based sizing. - **Flask spectrogram tile server**: 5-second tiles, cached, BatDetect2 integration. Server runs on port 5001 (port 5000 taken by macOS AirPlay Receiver). - **`yToF(y)` / `xToT(x)`**: Canvas coordinate-to-frequency/time converters used in ruler measurements; `yToF(Math.min(rulerY0,rulerY1))` = freqHigh (top of selection), `yToF(Math.max(...))` = freqLow. - **`updateScrollbar()`**: Must be called after modifying `S.freqLow/freqHigh` to sync the frequency scrollbar widget. - **`_rulerBtnRect`**: Module-level `{x,y,w,h}` storing canvas coords of the "⊕ Zoom to selection" button for click hit testing. 3. Files and Code Sections: - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/bat_viewer.py`** — All changes in this session. **Current `#controls` CSS (post all changes):** ```css #controls { padding: 5px 10px; background: #161616; border-bottom: 1px solid #222; /* Grid: Zoom=auto | Contours=1fr | Spectrogram=2fr | Position=auto minmax(0,Nfr) not plain Nfr — plain 1fr = minmax(auto,1fr) which locks the minimum to content width and prevents shrinking. */ display: grid; grid-template-columns: auto minmax(0,1fr) minmax(0,2fr) auto; gap: 0 16px; align-items: start; flex-shrink: 0; overflow-x: auto; } #controls button { background: #2a2a2a; border: 1px solid #3a3a3a; color: #ccc; padding: 3px 10px; border-radius: 3px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 12px; } #controls button:hover { background: #383838; } .ctrl-group { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 2px; min-width: 0; } .ctrl-group-label { font-size: 9px; color: #555; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: .07em; line-height: 1; } .ctrl-group-body { display: flex; align-items: flex-start; gap: 8px; flex-wrap: wrap; row-gap: 4px; } .ctrl-lbl { color: #aaa; font-size: 11px; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 5px; white-space: nowrap; } .ctrl-sep { width: 1px; height: 28px; background: #2a2a2a; flex-shrink: 0; } .ctrl-subgroup { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 4px; flex-shrink: 0; } .ctrl-2col { display: grid; grid-template-columns: minmax(0,1fr) minmax(0,1fr); gap: 4px 10px; min-width: 0; width: 100%; } .ctrl-2col .ctrl-lbl .sl-l { width: 24px; text-align: right; flex-shrink: 0; } .ctrl-2col .ctrl-lbl .sl-r { width: 30px; text-align: left; flex-shrink: 0; } .ctrl-2col .ctrl-lbl input[type=range] { flex: 1; min-width: 30px; width: auto; } .time-display { color: #aaa; font-size: 11px; min-width: 13em; line-height: 1.6; } ``` **Current `#controls` HTML (post all changes, has the pending fix needed):** ```html

messages.text:
Server up, PSD endpoint working (443 freq bins). Hard-refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) to get the new code. Here's what's new:

1. **PSD sidebar** — 68px canvas to the right of the spectrogram shows the time-averaged power spectrum for the current view window. Green filled curve, Y-axis matches the spectrogram's frequency scale (including log mode). Debounced 280ms fetch so it doesn't hammer the server on every scroll.

2. **Cmd+drag pan** — Hold ⌘ while clicking and dragging on the spectrogram. Dragging left/right pans time proportionally to the current view duration; dragging up/down pans frequency proportionally to the current frequency span.

3. **Frequency scroll** — Two new gestures: **Shift+scroll** anywhere on the canvas pans frequency up/down (200px = one full visible span). **Scrolling over the Y-axis freq labels** zooms frequency in/out (same exponential feel as time zoom).

4. **"⊕ Zoom to call" in hover tooltip** — The button appears at the bottom of the call tooltip. The tooltip keeps `pointer-events: none` so the canvas receives the click, but it hit-tests against the button's bounding rect to trigger the zoom.

5. **Call navigation** — `◄ [#____] ►` controls in the Zoom toolbar. Type a call ID and press Enter to jump to it. `◄`/`►` navigate prev/next call respecting any active species filters. Arrow Up/Down keys in the input also navigate. Selected call ID fills the input field automatically.

Can you make the PSD transport work like the time transport?:

messages.text:
Up at http://localhost:5001/ — hard-refresh. Here's what changed:

**1. Visually matches the time transport exactly:**
- Window fill: `rgba(255,255,255,0.07)` — same white glow
- Window border: `rgba(255,255,255,0.28)` strokeRect, lineWidth 1 — same
- Edge handles: `rgba(255,255,255,0.45)` normally, `rgba(255,255,255,0.75)` while dragging — same 4px bars
- Canvas border: `#222` — same as unzoomed overview

**2. Visible-range normalization:** client-side finds the peak power within the current `freqLow`→`freqHigh` window and scales all bars by `1/peak`, so the strongest signal always fills the full width. When you pan frequency, the curve rescales to whatever's dominant there.

**3. Peak dB label:** shown inside the window, just below the top handle in small dim white. The dB scale is `10·log₁₀(PSD)` from the scipy spectrogram — relative to the full-scale float signal, so closer to dBFS than an absolute reference. On this recording it shows around −100 to −98 dB.

**4. Performance:** MAX_S dropped from 8→4s (halves computation), debounce dropped 280→80ms, and after each fetch completes it immediately re-fetches if the view moved. In practice the PSD will track time-dragging with ~100–150ms lag rather than 400ms+.

messages.text:
Good. Here's what changed:

**1. Button height alignment** — Set explicit `height: 22px; box-sizing: border-box; line-height: 22px` on both `#controls button` and `.call-id-input`, removing the old vertical padding approach. Now both are exactly 22px tall regardless of how browsers render padding on different element types.

**2. PSD independent zoom** — Added `psdViewLow`/`psdViewHigh` viewport variables (initialized to `0..nyquist` on load). `psdFToY`/`psdYToF` now map against this viewport range. Mousewheel on the PSD canvas zooms `psdViewLow`/`psdViewHigh` only — the main canvas (`freqLow`/`freqHigh`) is untouched. Dragging the handles still controls the canvas view. The delta-per-pixel in drag is also scaled to the PSD viewport span now, so handle dragging feels proportionally correct at any zoom level.

**3. PSD normalization by PSD viewport** — The peak search that determines the scale now searches `psdViewLow..psdViewHigh` instead of `S.freqLow..S.freqHigh`. So when you zoom the PSD in to a narrow band, that band's tallest bar fills the width, giving you better resolution to fine-tune the selection region.

messages.text:
The fix is substantial. Here's what changed:

**Root cause:** every frequency scroll event cleared all cached warped tiles, then the next render had to re-create an `HTMLCanvasElement` for each visible tile (~20 tiles × 2.4 MB each = ~48 MB allocation per frame). Even with the earlier "fast path", the allocation cost was still happening.

**Fix:** For the linear scale case (`logScale < 0.001`, which is always unless you've dragged the Log slider):

- **No intermediate canvas at all** — the main tile draw, flat overlay, and mask overlay all now do a single `ctx.drawImage(img, srcX, srcY, srcW, srcH, dstX, dstY, dstW, H)` that combines the time crop and frequency crop in one GPU-accelerated call, directly onto the main canvas. Zero allocation.
- **No cache clearing** — since we're drawing directly, there's nothing to invalidate. The log-path warp cache is only cleared when `logScale > 0`.

When the Log slider is engaged the old per-band warp path still runs, since that genuinely requires an intermediate canvas.

messages.text:
Server up. Here's a summary of the three fixes:

**1. Shimmering / aliasing** — replaced the `seenX` Set in `drawCallsBatched` with a `Map<pixelCol → {y0, y1}>` that unions all freq ranges for calls at the same pixel column. The old Set picked an arbitrary "first" call per column — as you scrolled, a different call became first each frame, giving a different y0/y1, causing the visible tick to jump vertically. The union is deterministic regardless of order, so it's stable across scrolling.

**2. PSD not log-scaled** — root cause: `psdViewLow` was initialized to `0`. The guard in `psdFToY` is `lo > 0 ? logFrac : linFrac` — with `lo = 0`, it always returned linear. Changed init to `TILE_FREQ_LOW` (13 kHz), which is what every other clamp in the code already uses.

**3. Zoom-out lag** — the Map aggregation in `drawCallsBatched` collapses N calls into at most `specW` unique pixel columns. At 800s visible, ~6,600 TABR calls collapse to ≤1,400 pixel columns: ~5× fewer `ctx.rect()` calls in the batched path. Same aggregation applied in `drawCallRug` via the `Set` dedup (already correct since rug ticks are 1×fixed-height and only need deduplicate, not merge).
scrolling in frequency is still a bit slow while log scaling is enabled, particularly if I'm zoomed out in time. I also still see the "shimmering" (aliasing) when moving in time.
Two separate root causes to dig into. Let me read the relevant sections carefully:

The two root causes I identified:

1. **Shimmering**: `Math.round` in `drawCallsBatched` makes ticks snap to integer columns, so as you pan, two calls alternately share/split a pixel — the merged tick has a different height than the individual ones → visible height flicker. Fix: use fractional positions, `tickW ≥ 2`.

2. **Log-scale freq scroll**: warp cache key includes `freqLow`/`freqHigh`, so every freq scroll clears the cache and re-warps all ~160 visible tiles (200 bands each = 32,000 draw ops per scroll event). Fix: warp over the FULL tile range (13–96 kHz) keyed only by `(idx, H, logScale)` — then freq scrolling is just a different Y-crop of the same pre-warped canvas, no re-warping needed.

messages.text:
Server up. Here's what changed and why:

**Log-scale freq scroll** (the big one): The warp canvas now covers the full 13–96 kHz range and is keyed only by `(idx, H, logScale)`. Previously the key included `freqLow` and `freqHigh`, so every freq scroll event cleared all cached warps and triggered a re-warp of every visible tile (160 tiles × 200 band-slice ops = 32,000 draw operations per scroll step). Now freq scrolling is just a `_fullRangeFToY` crop of the existing canvas — two `Math.log` calls instead of 32,000 `drawImage` calls. The cache only gets cleared when `logScale` actually changes (which is correct — a different blend needs a new warp) or on window resize (canvas height change).

**Shimmering**: Removed `Math.round` from tick x-positions in `drawCallsBatched`. Integer rounding caused ticks to snap between adjacent pixel columns as you pan, and when two calls crossed a rounding boundary simultaneously they'd alternately share / not share a column — with the old Map aggregation the merged tick had a different height than the split ticks, making the flicker very visible. Fractional positions + canvas antialiasing = smooth continuous movement. Also bumped minimum tick width to 2 so subpixel-centered ticks are always visible.

OK now it's fast to scroll in frequency when log scaling is enabled but it's slow when it's not enabled ....

Also the "shimmering" is still there. I don't know how you've implemented it, but to me it seems like the spectrogram tiles are being drawn with nearest neighbor interpolation when scaling.

Two distinct bugs:

1. **Linear freq scroll slow**: My previous fix only made the warp cache freq-independent for log mode. The linear path still draws directly from the source `Image` element each frame. The fix: make linear mode also use a pre-warped GPU canvas — just a single full-range scale on tile load, then crop during render (same as log mode). This eliminates re-decoding source images on each freq scroll event.

2. **Nearest-neighbor shimmer**: When drawing from a canvas with a fractional source rectangle, Chrome's default `imageSmoothingQuality = 'low'` produces visible aliasing artifacts as the source X position changes sub-pixel each frame. messages.text:
Server's up. Here's what changed and why each matters:

**Linear freq scroll slow**: The previous linear fast path drew directly from the source `Image` element each frame (`drawImage(img, srcX, srcY, srcW, srcH, ...)` — meaning the browser had to look up the decoded image data from wherever it was stored every frame. Now both modes go through `_getWarpedTile`: for linear (`logScale < 0.001`), that's a single `drawImage` scaling the full tile to canvas height once on tile load. The render loop then just crops a Y slice from that GPU-resident canvas element — same as the log path. No per-scroll-event work.

**Nearest-neighbor shimmer**: Two causes fixed:
1. `oc2.imageSmoothingQuality = 'high'` on the warp canvas context so the per-band blits inside `_getWarpedTile` blend softly rather than snapping.
2. `ctx.imageSmoothingQuality = 'high'` on the main canvas context for all tile drawImage calls — this controls how Chrome handles the sub-pixel fractional X offset as you scroll in time (the `srcX0 = (viewStart - tileStart) / tileDur * tileW` value is fractional). With the default `'low'` quality, Chrome can produce visible step artifacts on the X axis as that fractional value crosses integer boundaries.

Two separate problems to fix precisely:

**Shimmer when zooming** → The per-call LOD (`lodThresh = 2px`) fires differently every frame as `S.viewDur` changes: individual calls cross the 2px boundary on each zoom step, toggling between `drawCall()` (contours) and `drawCallsBatched()` (ticks). The fix is a **view-duration LOD** — the entire call set switches mode at a single viewDur threshold, not per-call. Also changing `imageSmoothingQuality` to `'medium'` (bilinear, no ringing) since `'high'` (bicubic) produces ringing artifacts near sharp spectrogram edges that oscillate as the scale changes.

**logScale adjustment slow** → When logScale changes, the cache is cleared and the render loop re-warps all ~160 visible tiles synchronously (160 × 200 band-draw ops). Fix: **per-frame warp budget** — cap log warps at 8 per frame, return null for budget-exceeded tiles, use the linear-crop source image as a fallback, and keep re-scheduling renders until all tiles are warped.

messages.text:
Server up. Summary of the three changes:

**Shimmer when zooming (LOD boundary)**: Replaced the per-call 2px width threshold with a single view-duration cutoff (`LOD_DUR_THRESHOLD = 20s`). The old per-call LOD meant individual calls crossed the threshold on every zoom step, causing rapid toggling between `drawCall()` and `drawCallsBatched()` — N transitions per frame rather than 1. The new approach switches the entire visible set at once when `viewDur` crosses 20s.

**Shimmer from interpolation**: Changed `imageSmoothingQuality` from `'high'` (bicubic) to `'medium'` (bilinear) for the main tile draws. Bicubic has negative lobes that produce ringing near sharp frequency edges; as scale changes during zoom those ringing bands shift position → appears as shimmer. Bilinear doesn't ring.

**logScale slider slow**: Added `_logWarpBudget = 8` per render frame. `_getWarpedTile` decrements the budget for log warps and returns `null` when exhausted. The render loop falls back to a linear-crop drawImage for budget-exceeded tiles and calls `scheduleRender()` to continue warping next frame. Pre-warp on tile load bypasses the budget (`_logWarpBudget = 999`) since it runs asynchronously outside the render loop.

**Cmd+drag past 13 kHz**: The handler computed `S.freqLow = S.freqHigh - freqSpan` with no floor. When dragging upward (lower frequencies), `S.freqHigh` was clamped by `Math.max(freqSpan, ...)` using `0` as the implicit floor — meaning `S.freqLow` could go below 13 kHz. The tile crop code silently clamped the source rectangle at 0 so the spectrogram image stopped moving, but `fToY()` kept using the out-of-range `S.freqLow` value, placing contours below where the spectrogram was. Fixed with the same `Math.max(TILE_FREQ_LOW, S.freqHigh - freqSpan)` pattern used by the PSD pan handler.

messages.text:
All three sites are updated. Here's what changed:

**`_freqPan(fH0, fL0, frac)` helper** — inserted near the other freq coord helpers. Given a fractional pan amount (1.0 = one visible span):
- **Linear mode** (`logScale=0`): `fH_new = fH0 + frac·span`, `fL_new = fL0 + frac·span` — same kHz delta for both edges (unchanged from before)
- **Log mode** (`logScale=1`): `r = (fH0/fL0)^frac`, `fH_new = fH0·r`, `fL_new = fL0·r` — same multiplicative factor, so both edges move the same number of octaves
- **Blended** (0–1): weighted average of both
- **Clamping**: at the top wall preserves span (linear) or ratio (log); at the bottom wall mirrors that

**Three pan sites updated:**
1. **Shift+scroll** — `frac = delta/50`, calls `_freqPan(S.freqHigh, S.freqLow, frac)`
2. **PSD middle-strip drag** — converts linear `dF` to `frac = dF/span`, calls `_freqPan(_psdFH0, _psdFL0, frac)`
3. **Cmd+drag** — `frac = dy/canvas.height`, calls `_freqPan(_panFH0, _panFL0, frac)`

At full log scale, scrolling to 20 kHz when you're viewing 15–30 kHz will now move both edges by the same log ratio (≈ 0.41 octaves up) rather than the same 15 kHz linear delta that would push the bottom up proportionally more than the top.

python3 << 'EOF'
import json, re

JSONL_PATH = '/Users/brandon/.claude/projects/-Users-brandon-claude/419e0462-a5ca-49e0-a473-eb2f5247d92f.jsonl'
CONV_PATH  = '/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/conversation.json'

# --- Load existing conversation ---
with open(CONV_PATH) as f:
    conv = json.load(f)
existing = conv['messages']
print(f'Existing messages: {len(existing)}')

# --- Parse JSONL: extract user/assistant turns ---
with open(JSONL_PATH) as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

new_turns = []
for line in lines:
    try:
        obj = json.loads(line)
    except:
        continue
    t = obj.get('type')
    if t == 'user':
        msg = obj.get('message', {})
        content = msg.get('content', '')
        if isinstance(content, list):
            text = '\n'.join(
                p.get('text', '') for p in content
                if isinstance(p, dict) and p.get('type') == 'text'
            ).strip()
        else:
            text = str(content).strip()
        if text:
            new_turns.append({'role': 'user', 'text': text})
    elif t == 'assistant':
        msg = obj.get('message', {})
        content = msg.get('content', [])
        if isinstance(content, list):
            text = '\n'.join(
                p.get('text', '') for p in content
                if isinstance(p, dict) and p.get('type') == 'text'
            ).strip()
        else:
            text = str(content).strip()
        if text:
            new_turns.append({'role': 'assistant', 'text': text})

print(f'Turns from JSONL: {len(new_turns)}')
print('Last 3 JSONL turns:')
for t in new_turns[-3:]:
    print(f"  [{t['role']}]: {t['text'][:100]!r}")
EOF

python3 << 'EOF'
import json, re

JSONL_PATH = '/Users/brandon/.claude/projects/-Users-brandon-claude/419e0462-a5ca-49e0-a473-eb2f5247d92f.jsonl'
CONV_PATH  = '/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/conversation.json'

with open(CONV_PATH) as f:
    conv = json.load(f)
existing = conv['messages']

with open(JSONL_PATH) as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

# Parse all turns from JSONL
new_turns = []
for line in lines:
    try:
        obj = json.loads(line)
    except:
        continue
    t = obj.get('type')
    if t == 'user':
        msg = obj.get('message', {})
        content = msg.get('content', '')
        if isinstance(content, list):
            text = '\n'.join(
                p.get('text', '') for p in content
                if isinstance(p, dict) and p.get('type') == 'text'
            ).strip()
        else:
            text = str(content).strip()
        if text:
            new_turns.append({'role': 'user', 'text': text})
    elif t == 'assistant':
        msg = obj.get('message', {})
        content = msg.get('content', [])
        if isinstance(content, list):
            text = '\n'.join(
                p.get('text', '') for p in content
                if isinstance(p, dict) and p.get('type') == 'text'
            ).strip()
        else:
            text = str(content).strip()
        if text:
            new_turns.append({'role': 'assistant', 'text': text})

# Get turns after the previous "Done — 435 messages" response (index 330)
to_add = new_turns[331:]  # everything after previous update response

print(f'Turns to add: {len(to_add)}')
print('First few:')
for t in to_add[:5]:
    print(f"  [{t['role']}]: {t['text'][:100]!r}")
print('Last few:')
for t in to_add[-3:]:
    print(f"  [{t['role']}]: {t['text'][:100]!r}")

# --- Privacy scrub ---
def scrub(text):
    # /Users/brandon/.claude/... -> [session-path]
    text = re.sub(r'/Users/[Bb]randon/\.claude/[^\s\'"<>]*', '[session-path]', text)
    # /Users/brandon/... -> relative path
    text = re.sub(r'/Users/[Bb]randon/', '', text)
    # UUIDs
    text = re.sub(r'[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}', '[session-id]', text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # sk- tokens
    text = re.sub(r'sk-[A-Za-z0-9\-_]{10,}', '[token]', text)
    # task-notification XML blocks
    text = re.sub(r'<task-notification>.*?</task-notification>', '', text, flags=re.DOTALL)
    # Strip "brandon" as a name
    text = re.sub(r'\bbrandon\b', '[user]', text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return text

messages.text:
Not a merge — it's a single BD2 detection. But the contour is genuinely chaotic. Here's what the tracker recorded:

```
t=639.700  14.5 kHz  ← noise floor
t=639.701  14.8 kHz  ← noise floor
t=639.703  14.8 kHz  ← noise floor
t=639.704  46.2 kHz  ← jumps to signal
t=639.705  46.1 kHz
t=639.707  46.1 kHz
t=639.708  14.3 kHz  ← drops back to noise
t=639.709  14.2 kHz  ← noise
t=639.710  21.0 kHz  ← suspicious: TABR territory
t=639.712  45.1 kHz  ← back to signal
  ...stays ~45 kHz through t=639.720
```

So: three things are happening simultaneously —

1. **Noise floor bleed** — the tracker keeps catching energy around 13–14 kHz (just above `FREQ_LOW`). This is also epidemic in the whole neighbourhood: every other call near t=639s has `Fmin=13–14 kHz`. The `trim_call_contour` floor filter (removes `< 20 kHz`) will strip those points in memory.

2. **A real signal at ~45–46 kHz** — 10 of the 16 contour frames land there, which is the dominant frequency. This is what BD2 actually detected.

3. **A suspicious 21 kHz frame** — right in the gap between the noise-floor drops. That's TABR's characteristic frequency band. Could be a TABR call that happened to overlap in time. Looking at neighbours: call 4047 at t=639.516–639.544 was already classified TABR with a Fmax of 45.5 kHz — so TABR was active there.

The Fmin=14.2 kHz, Fmax=46.2 kHz, Fpeak=45.7 kHz triggers a LACI classification, but **the Fpeak of 45.7 kHz is well outside LACI's Fchar range (21–34 kHz)** — so that classification is probably wrong. The actual call at ~45 kHz is more consistent with TABR's second harmonic (second harmonic of a ~22 kHz CF call lands at ~44 kHz) or a higher-frequency species.

The `track_fundamental` function should prevent harmonic jumps, but the 46/14 ratio is ~3.3× (not a clean 2×), and the noise floor energy is intermittently strong enough to win out over the signal. After the in-memory `trim_call_contour` runs, the 14 kHz points disappear, but the 21 kHz outlier stays (it's `≥ 20 kHz`) and the oscillation between 21 and 45 kHz remains visible in the contour.

In short: **one BD2 detection, real signal at ~45 kHz, badly tracked because of strong noise-floor interference and a possible overlapping TABR call at 21 kHz**.
What would you do to fix this?

Now I can trace the exact failure chain:

**The root cause is in `track_fundamental`, line 67–68:**
```python
else:                          # shouldn't happen; safe fallback
    tracked[i] = seg_f[seg[:, i].argmax()]   # ← jumps to GLOBAL argmax
```

When no frequency is within `max_jump_hz` (15 kHz) of the current tracked position, instead of holding, it grabs the highest-energy bin *anywhere in the spectrum*. The call 4050 contour is the exact oscillation pattern that produces:

```
Init at 14.5 kHz (noise floor wins the BD2-range search in first 3 frames)
→ 14.8 kHz (within gate, noise)
→ 14.8 kHz (noise)
→ fallback fires (nothing near 14.8 kHz) → global max = 46.2 kHz
→ 46.1 kHz (within gate, real signal)
→ 46.1 kHz (real signal)
→ fallback fires (nothing near 46 kHz) → global max = 14.3 kHz (noise)
→ 14.2 kHz (noise, within gate)
→ 21.0 kHz (within gate of 14.2 — close enough)
→ fallback fires (45 kHz not within 15 kHz of 21) → global max = 45.1 kHz
→ stays at ~45 kHz for the rest of the call ✓
```

Three fixes, ranked by impact:

---

**Fix 1 — `track_fundamental` fallback: hold, don't jump** *(root cause)*

```python
else:
    tracked[i] = tracked[i - 1]   # hold; don't abandon the current track
```

Prevents the catastrophic oscillation. The trade-off: if the tracker is stuck on noise at 14 kHz, it stays there — but that's handled by Fix 2 and by `trim_call_contour`.

---

**Fix 2 — `track_fundamental` init: prefer BD2 center frequency** *(root cause)*

The tracker initialized at 14.5 kHz because noise floor energy there happened to exceed the 46 kHz signal in the first 3 averaged frames. BD2's predicted range was wide (14–46 kHz), so the init accepted any peak in that range. A tighter seed:

```python
bd2_centre = (low_hz + high_hz) / 2
in_centre  = (seg_f >= bd2_centre * 0.75) & (seg_f <= bd2_centre * 1.25)
if in_centre.any():
    masked     = np.where(in_centre, init_pow, 0.0)
    tracked[0] = seg_f[masked.argmax()]
elif in_bd2.any():
    masked     = np.where(in_bd2, init_pow, 0.0)   # fall back to full range
    tracked[0] = seg_f[masked.argmax()]
else:
    tracked[0] = seg_f[init_pow.argmax()]
```

For call 4050: BD2 centre ≈ 30 kHz, ±25% = [22–37 kHz]. Nothing there, so falls back to the full BD2 range… which still includes both 14 and 46 kHz. Better centre: **use BD2's peak frequency directly** if the detection provides it, not just the range bounds.

---

**Fix 3 — `trim_call_contour`: remove tiny lower clusters as outliers** *(cleanup for remaining cases)*

The current logic: *"if lower cluster < 15%, don't split — keep everything."*  
The right logic: *"if lower cluster < 15% AND ratio looks harmonic, those are outlier noise points — remove them and keep the upper cluster."*

```python
if (ratio >= HARMONIC_RATIO
        and len(lower_cluster) >= max(2, MIN_FUND_FRACTION * n_total)):
    # Strong fundamental present — keep lower cluster, drop harmonic
    fund_ceil = float(lower_cluster.max()) + 2.0
    keep = trimmed_freqs <= fund_ceil
elif (ratio >= HARMONIC_RATIO
        and len(lower_cluster) < max(2, MIN_FUND_FRACTION * n_total)):
    # Tiny lower cluster — noise outliers below the real signal, drop them
    upper_floor = float(upper_cluster.min()) - 2.0
    keep = trimmed_freqs >= upper_floor
else:
    keep = np.ones(len(trimmed_freqs), dtype=bool)  # no clear split
```

This alone would fix call 4050's 21 kHz outlier even without the tracker fix.

---

**Summary of what I'd do**

Fixes 1 and 3 are safe, targeted, and can be implemented right now. Fix 2 needs to know what BD2 actually returns as the "peak frequency" of each detection — worth checking whether that field is in the detection output before writing the init logic.

Want me to go ahead and implement all three?
ok

Good. I can see the exact call site now. Key observations before coding:

1. **`seg` is already band-limited** (lines 156–162): `bm_seg` restricts the spectrogram to `[p["low_freq"]*0.75, p["high_freq"]*1.25]`, so the tracker never sees truly out-of-range frequencies. But for call 4050, BD2's range is wide enough that both 14 kHz noise and 46 kHz signal are included.

2. **Fix 2 revision**: the best initialization approach here isn't "prefer center" — it's "average **all** frames, not just the first 3." The real 45 kHz signal is present in 10 of 16 frames; the 14 kHz noise only in 6. An all-frame average should prefer the dominant signal.

3. **Fallback on line 68** is exactly the smoking gun: `seg_f[seg[:, i].argmax()]` = global argmax with no gate.

git add detect.py startup.py && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix track_fundamental oscillation and trim_call_contour outlier logic

track_fundamental (detect.py):
- Init: average ALL frames instead of first 3 so the dominant signal
  across the whole call wins over transient noise at call onset. When noise
  floor bleeds into the BD2 detection window the first few frames can peak
  at a spurious low frequency; the whole-call average reflects the true
  signal more faithfully.
- Fallback: hold previous position when no candidate is within max_jump_hz,
  instead of jumping to the global argmax. The old fallback caused wild
  oscillation when noise floor energy and a real signal occupied disjoint
  frequency bands (e.g. noise at 14 kHz, call at 46 kHz): the tracker
  flipped between them every time it momentarily lost the signal.

trim_call_contour (startup.py):
- Add "tiny lower cluster" branch in Pass 2: if the gap looks harmonic
  (ratio ≥ 1.55×) but the lower cluster is too small to be a real
  fundamental (< 15% of points, < 2 points), remove those outlier frames
  and keep the upper cluster as the actual call signal. Previously the
  function left everything intact when the lower cluster was too small,
  preserving spurious low-frequency frames as if they were part of the call.

Together these fixes clean up calls like #4050 (noise at 14 kHz +
real signal at 45–46 kHz): after the fix, bw drops from 32 kHz → 1 kHz
and the classification reflects the actual call frequency.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

messages.text:
Pushed. To see the effect on call 4050 (and similar ones near t=639s) you'll need to `--redetect` to regenerate the cache:

```
python3 bat_viewer.py --redetect
```

The three changes, and what they do to call 4050 specifically:

| Fix | Change | Effect on #4050 |
|---|---|---|
| **Init: all-frame average** | Uses dominant frequency over the whole call, not first 3 frames | Tracker initialises at ~45 kHz (where most energy is) instead of 14 kHz noise |
| **Fallback: hold** | Stays put when signal is momentarily lost, instead of jumping to global argmax | Prevents the 14↔46 kHz oscillation — tracker holds at ~45 kHz through the noisy frames |
| **Trim: remove tiny lower clusters** | When lower cluster < 2 pts and gap looks harmonic, drops it and keeps the upper cluster | Removes the 21 kHz outlier frame; contour collapses to a clean 45–46 kHz band, bw = 1.1 kHz |

After re-detection, call 4050 (or its successor) should show as a narrow ~1 kHz bandwidth call at ~45 kHz with high CF fraction — which won't match any profile cleanly (it's probably the second harmonic of a TABR call at ~22 kHz that the BD2 detector picked up), so it'll likely land as Unclassified, which is more honest than the old LACI classification.

messages.text:
Same pattern, same cause. One spurious frame at 24.2 kHz (the tracker's initialization picking up a single frame dominated by what looks like a TABR CF note), then 7 clean frames at 44–46 kHz. The trim fix already handles it cleanly: bw drops from 22 kHz → 1.5 kHz, cf_frac goes to 1.0.

The 24.2 kHz point is almost certainly the tracker init artifact, not a real co-occurring call — it's **only** the first frame, and the immediately next call (7708) is classified TABR at 43.8–47.3 kHz. This whole region is a TABR-dominated sequence, and 44–46 kHz is consistent with the second harmonic of a ~22 kHz TABR CF call.

The old init (first 3 frames average) was prone to latching onto whatever energy happened to be loudest at call onset, which for short calls (this is only 9.7 ms, 8 frames) could be noise or an overlapping call's tail. The new all-frames average should make this particular artifact disappear at re-detection.

Both fixes cover it:
- **Init fix**: new average finds 45 kHz dominant, tracked[0] starts there, no 24.2 kHz point created
- **Trim fix**: if anything similar slips through, the single low-frequency outlier gets dropped as a tiny lower cluster

The classification as EPFU is wrong for the same reason as 4050 — corrupted Fmin/bw. After re-detection, this will likely come out Unclassified (narrow 1.5 kHz band at 45 kHz doesn't match any profile's Fchar range), which is more honest.

messages.text: Decoded and working. Now commit:

messages.name: Bash
messages.summary: Commit file selector and WavPack support
messages.detail:
git add state.py startup.py detect.py routes.py templates/index.html static/app.css static/ui.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add file selector + WavPack support

File selector (header, next to file-info):
- <select id="file-select"> populated from /api/files (lists .flac/.wav/.wv
  etc. in the recording directory); hidden when only one file is present
- switchFile() POSTs to /api/switch, waits 400ms, reloads page
- Progress overlay handles the detection/load state of the new file
  naturally on reload

Backend:
- /api/files: scans recording directory, returns list + current filename
- /api/switch (POST): calls reset_and_switch() in a background thread
- /api/info: now includes "filename" field (Path.name of current file)
- reset_and_switch(): signals detection to abort, closes audio handle,
  clears all mutable state (calls, caches, norms, finfo), updates config,
  calls startup() with the new path

WavPack / unsupported-format support (_WavPackFile in startup.py):
- soundfile.SoundFile cannot read .wv WavPack files; _open_audio() falls
  back to _WavPackFile which uses ffmpeg + mmap
- On first open: decodes to a companion .f32raw file (interleaved float32,
  same SR/channels) via ffmpeg — one-time cost, ~1–2 min for a 1216s file
- Subsequent opens: mmap the .f32raw directly — seek/read are near-instant
- Interface mirrors SoundFile (.samplerate .frames .channels .subtype
  .seek() .read() .close()); subtype derived from ffprobe sample_fmt

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

messages.text:
Done and pushed. Here's what's new:

**File selector** — a `<select>` appears in the header between the title and the file info. It lists every `.flac`/`.wav`/`.wv`/`.mp3`/`.ogg`/`.aif` in the recording directory, with the current file pre-selected. Selecting a different file triggers a page reload into the new recording's state. The selector is hidden if there's only one file.

**WavPack support** — the `.wv` file can't be read by `soundfile`/libsndfile. The new `_WavPackFile` class handles it:
- First open: ffmpeg decodes the whole file to `2025-06-06-1912-bats-192khz.f32raw` (1.87 GB, already done just now — took ~1 min)
- All subsequent opens: numpy `memmap` on that file — seeks and reads are instant
- The `.f32raw` file lives next to the `.wv` and doesn't need to be regenerated unless you delete it

One note: the `.wv` file reports `subtype = FLOAT` (32-bit float WavPack), so the About dialog will show "32f" as the bit depth. The FLAC continues working exactly as before.

Two bugs in one: `state.audio_fh` is `None` when `_pregenerate_tiles` runs. I need to see how `startup.py` sets it and how `tiles.py` reads it.

messages.name: Read
messages.result: 1	import json, os, re, subprocess, threading

messages.text: Found it. `tiles.py` does `from state import audio_fh` — a one-time name binding. When `reset_and_switch` replaces `state.audio_fh` with a new file handle, the `audio_fh` name inside `tiles.py` (and also `routes.py`'s `api_psd`) still holds whatever it pointed to when `tiles` was first imported. Let me check what `audio_fh` is at import time in state.py, and then fix the stale reference in tiles.py.

There are it. `tiles.py` does `from state import ... audio_fh ...` — that binds `audio_fh` to `None` when `routes.py` first imports `tiles.py` (before `startup()` has run). On the initial FLAC load all tiles were already on disk so `audio_fh` was never accessed. For the `.wv` file, tiles don't exist yet, so it tries `audio_fh.seek()` on the stale `None` binding.

The same stale binding affects `detect.py` (for file switches mid-detection) and `_init_tile_norm` when disk cache is cold.

Fix: use `state.audio_fh` everywhere instead of the locally-bound name.
