So there will be several TCP calls each time it has so fetch a new segment? Is this the best way, or are there better ways to do streaming (websockets, streaming protocols?)
[Request interrupted by user]
So there will be several HTTP requests each time it has so fetch a new segment? Is this the best way, or are there better ways to do streaming (websockets, streaming protocols?)

Good question — and honestly, for this specific use case, multiple HTTP requests are fine and arguably the right choice. Here's why, and where the alternatives actually win:

## Why HTTP requests are fine here

The request frequency is very low because you're playing slowly:

| Playback rate | Source frames consumed/sec | 1-second chunk lasts |
|---|---|---|
| 1x | 192,000 | 1 sec real time |
| 1/16x | 12,000 | 16 sec real time |
| 1/32x | 6,000 | 32 sec real time |

At 1/16x you're making maybe 4 requests per minute. And HTTP/1.1 keep-alive (Flask's default) reuses the TCP connection — there's no new handshake per chunk, just ~400 bytes of headers each way. Negligible.

## Where WebSockets would actually help

WebSockets make sense when:
- **Request frequency is high** — e.g. 1x playback with tiny chunks, or a game sending input 60x/sec
- **Server needs to push unsolicited data** — e.g. notifying all clients when detection finishes
- **Bidirectional low-latency comms** — e.g. collaborative editing

For audio streaming where the server is just a dumb byte-range dispenser, WebSockets add stateful connections on the server for no real gain.

## HTTP streaming (chunked transfer) — the middle ground

There's a third option you didn't mention: one persistent HTTP response with `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`, read client-side via `response.body.getReader()`:

```
GET /api/audio_stream?f=<fid>&start=<frame>
← streams forever until client closes
```

Single connection, server pushes data at a controlled rate, no per-chunk request overhead. Seeking closes the stream and opens a new one. This is genuinely slightly cleaner than multiple range requests, and Flask supports it with a generator response. But the difference is minor at low playback rates.

## What the big players actually do

- **Spotify / Apple Music**: HTTP range requests, chunked, over HTTP/2 (which multiplexes them over one TCP connection with compressed headers — essentially free per-request overhead)
- **YouTube**: DASH or HLS — HTTP range requests for encoded video segments, MSE on the client
- **WebRTC audio**: UDP, designed for real-time two-way calls — totally wrong tool here

## Honest recommendation for your case

Stick with HTTP range requests. The only change worth making is if you later want to run under **Hypercorn or Gunicorn + h2** instead of Flask's dev server, which would give you HTTP/2 multiplexing for free. But for a local tool with slow playback rates and a handful of users, it genuinely doesn't matter.

The only scenario where I'd reach for WebSockets here is if you wanted the server to **push a "detection complete" notification** or **sync playback position across multiple browser tabs** — but that's a different feature, not a streaming concern.
What would you do to avoid interruptions to playback from buffering

The core principle is that the AudioWorklet thread must never wait for a fetch. Every strategy follows from that.

## The fundamental rule: decouple fetching from playback completely

The worklet reads from a ring buffer. A separate fetch worker writes to it. The worklet should never know or care whether a fetch is in progress — it just reads until the buffer is empty, and if that happens, outputs silence and signals an underrun.

```
Fetch worker ──writes──> [ring buffer] ──reads──> AudioWorklet ──> speakers
                              ↑
                         Main thread monitors fill level,
                         triggers fetches when it drops
```

## Specific techniques

**1. Buffer for worst-case rate, not current rate**

The buffer should always hold enough source audio to cover the maximum playback speed (1x), not the current slow rate. At 1x you consume ~768 KB/sec (192kHz mono float32). Keep 10 seconds buffered = ~7.5 MB. On a local server that fetch takes <50ms. At 1/16x that same buffer covers 160 seconds of real playback time — you have minutes of headroom.

**2. Fetch well before the buffer drains**

Trigger a fetch when the buffer reaches 50% full, not 0%. This gives you the entire second half of the buffer as recovery time if the fetch is delayed. Never fetch reactively — always predictively.

**3. Keep the fetch worker isolated**

Fetches should happen on a dedicated Web Worker, not the main thread. The main thread can get blocked by layout, GC, or user interaction. A fetch worker is independent and keeps writing to the ring buffer regardless.

**4. Preallocate — avoid GC during playback**

Allocate all your `ArrayBuffer`s once at startup and reuse them. Allocating large buffers mid-playback can trigger a garbage collection pause that stalls the fetch worker (not the audio thread itself, but the writing side). With a preallocated ring buffer, there's nothing to GC.

**5. Silent underrun, not glitch**

If the worklet does run dry, output zeros (silence) rather than repeating the last sample or doing anything weird. Also post a message back to the main thread so it can respond — maybe showing a brief "buffering" indicator and triggering an emergency fetch. Silence is far less jarring than a glitch or click.

**6. Handle seeks with a brief intentional pause**

On seek: immediately halt playback, clear the ring buffer, start fetching from the new position. Don't resume until you have at least 1–2 seconds buffered. A deliberate 100–200ms pause after a seek is unnoticeable; a dropout 2 seconds in is.

**7. Rate changes need buffer reassessment**

When rate increases (e.g., 1/32x → 1/4x), consumption jumps 8x. The fetch worker needs to know the current rate to calculate how aggressively to prefetch. The simplest approach: size the buffer for 1x always, so any rate change is already covered.

## For a local server specifically

Network latency is essentially zero — a 10 MB fetch over loopback takes maybe 30ms. The real risks here aren't network jitter but:
- JavaScript GC pauses hitting the fetch worker
- The main thread blocking the fetch worker (solved by using a Worker)
- A seek or rate spike draining the buffer faster than anticipated

With a 10-second buffer and a fetch worker that refills at 50%, your margin on localhost is so large that interruptions would essentially never happen under normal conditions. The techniques above matter much more when you're streaming over a real network with variable latency.
How about server-side. Does the entire audio file need to be stored in memory? I have several files, each of which is > 1GB.

No — and your current implementation is already handling this correctly, mostly by accident.

## What `np.memmap` actually does

`np.memmap` calls `mmap()` under the hood, which maps the file into the process's virtual address space but loads **nothing into RAM**. The OS page cache handles everything:

- When code accesses `self._mmap[start:end]`, the OS pages in only those 4KB blocks from disk
- Pages that haven't been touched stay on disk
- Under memory pressure, the OS evicts cold pages automatically — they're backed by the file, so nothing is lost
- On next access, evicted pages are re-read from disk transparently

For a 1GB file with users scattered at different positions, only the ~few MB around each active playback position actually occupies RAM at any moment.

## The multi-process advantage

This becomes even better with a real server (gunicorn multi-worker). Each worker process has its own `mmap` handle pointing to the same file, but the OS page cache is shared system-wide across all processes. The data is physically in RAM **once** regardless of how many workers or users:

```
Worker 1 ──mmap──> page cache ──disk──> file.f32raw
Worker 2 ──mmap──>     ↑
Worker 3 ──mmap──>  (shared physical pages)
```

Compare that to loading into a numpy array: each gunicorn worker would hold its own full copy in RAM — 3 workers × 1GB = 3GB just for one file.

## What would be worse

A few patterns that would unnecessarily force data into RAM:

```python
# Bad — loads everything into RAM
data = np.fromfile("audio.f32raw", dtype="float32")

# Bad — same problem
with open("audio.f32raw", "rb") as f:
    data = f.read()

# Fine — mmap, OS manages pages
data = np.memmap("audio.f32raw", dtype="float32", mode="r")
```

## One real concern: the audio lock

Your current code has an `audio_lock` around seeks/reads on `entry.audio_fh`. For soundfile handles this is necessary (libsndfile is not thread-safe). But for mmap slice access it isn't — array indexing is naturally parallel and thread-safe for reads. Once you add an audio chunk endpoint, you'd want to read directly from `entry._mmap` rather than through the soundfile-style seek/read interface, bypassing the lock entirely.

## Disk speed

The only remaining concern is how fast pages come in from disk on a cache miss:

| Storage | Sequential read | Random 4KB read |
|---|---|---|
| NVMe SSD | ~3 GB/s | ~0.1ms |
| SATA SSD | ~500 MB/s | ~0.1ms |
| HDD | ~150 MB/s | ~5–10ms |

Even on an HDD, a cache miss during 1/16x playback gives you 16 seconds of real time to fetch the next disk page. You'd need to be on a very slow spinning disk with very high memory pressure before this ever caused an audible gap.

So: your architecture is already sound for multiple large files. The page cache is doing the right thing for free.

16 384 bytes = 4 096 frames × 4 bytes/float32. Everything is wired up. Here's a summary of what was implemented across 8 files:

**New files:**
- **`static/audio-worklet.js`** — AudioWorkletProcessor with linear-interpolation resampler. Reads `rateX1000` from SharedArrayBuffer and advances through the ring buffer at `step = (srcSr / sampleRate) * rate` source-frames per output sample. At 1/16×, a 40 kHz bat call sounds at ~2.5 kHz (audible).
- **`static/audio-worker.js`** — Fetch Web Worker that prefetches 0.5 s chunks from `/api/audio_chunk`, writing them into the SAB ring buffer up to 3 s ahead of the playhead. Handles seek messages by resetting ring-buffer pointers and restarting prefetch.
- **`static/player.js`** — Playback engine. `audioInit` primes the player for a file. `audioPlayPause` lazily creates the AudioContext (browser policy: must be user-gesture-initiated) on first play. A `requestAnimationFrame` loop reads the worklet's `readFrame` Atomic each frame to advance `S.playheadTime` and auto-scroll the viewport to follow playback.

**Modified files:**
- **`routes.py`** — Added `@app.after_request` COOP/COEP headers (required for `SharedArrayBuffer`) and `/api/audio_chunk` endpoint (serves raw mono float32 slices via soundfile, cap 1 s per request).
- **`state.js`** — Added `playheadTime: 0` and `isPlaying: false`.
- **`render.js`** — Added `drawPlayhead(W, H)` (bright `#00d488` line with triangle handle) called between ruler and crosshairs; also draws it in `drawOverview()`.
- **`events.js`** — Playhead drag (grab zone ±9 px), Space bar play/pause (skipped when focus is in a text input), Cmd+click on overview seeks without panning, `handleClick` advances playhead when a call is selected.
- **`ui.js`** — `navigateCall` and `jumpToCallId` both set `S.playheadTime` and call `audioSeek` if playing; `init()` calls `audioInit` and `_initRateSlider` after info loads.
- **`app.css`** — Controls grid widened to 5 columns; play button styled in playhead green (`#00d488`).
- **`templates/index.html`** — Playback control group added with ▶ button and 6-step rate slider (1/32×–1×, default 1/16×); `player.js` script tag added.

Good. Here's what each fix does:

1. **Controls layout** — Playback (▶ + speed slider) is now the first row of the rightmost *Position* column, with the time display below it. The CSS grid reverted to 4 columns, so nothing crowds.

2. **Double-click to seek** — `dblclick` on the spectrogram canvas places the playhead at that time. The single-click/drag/ruler behavior is unchanged.

3. **No more auto-scroll** — Removed the viewport-snap lines from `_startRAF`. The view stays where you left it; the playhead line moves through it.

4. **Seek position persisted on play** — Root cause was that the worklet's internal `this._pos` float64 is the only thing that controls read position; writing to the SAB `readFrame` word does nothing (the worklet overwrites it each block). Added `this.port.onmessage = ({ data }) => { if (data.type === 'seek') this._pos = data.frame; }` in the worklet constructor, and now `_audioPlay` and `audioSeek` both send that message before enabling playback.

5. **Click on start/seek at 1×** — Two-part fix: ① added `_waitForBuffer(startFrame)` which spins on the Atomics `writeFrame` word (16ms polls) and waits until the worker has prefetched ≥1 s before setting `ctrl[2]=1`; ② in the worklet, replaced the hard zero-fill on underrun with an exponential decay from the last valid sample (`gain *= 0.998` per sample ≈ −10 dB over 10 ms), so any residual underruns fade out rather than click.

messages.text:
The play button, slider, and rate label are now in the header. Here's what changed:

**Layout** — The header now has three flex sections: left (h1 + file-select), centre (`flex:1` with `justify-content:center` — the ▶ + slider + label), and right (CLASSIFIER + session + about). The centre section expands to fill remaining space and centres its contents, so the controls sit in the visual middle of the bar regardless of how wide the file name is.

**Continuous rate slider** — The slider is now `min=0 max=100 step=1`. The mapping is logarithmic: `rate = 2^(v/100 × 5 − 5)`, so the six snap points fall exactly at v = 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 (1/32×, 1/16×, 1/8×, 1/4×, 1/2×, 1×). Between snaps you can set any value — the label shows `×0.088` etc. When you drag within ±3 units of a snap stop the thumb visually jumps to it and the label shows the clean fraction.

1. Remove the "pick" slider. Set the default pick distance to 10px.
2. Widen the playback rate slider a bit.
3. Put the "Position: HH:MM:SS.sss" text next to the playback rate slider. To the right.
4. Replace "zoom in" "zoom out" "fit all" with magnifying glass +/- icons and fit all with a home icon. Put them to the left of the call navigation buttons so they're all on one line.

messages.text:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation. Summary: 1. Primary Request and Intent: **CRITICAL Security/Privacy Rule (preserve verbatim):** Before committing `conversation.json`, always scrub: `/Users/brandon/...` paths → relative paths, `/Users/brandon/.claude/...` → `[session-path]`, UUIDs → `[session-id]`, `` XML blocks, name "brandon", `sk-` tokens. Verify 0 hits before committing. The project is `xpabat` — a Flask + vanilla JS bat spectrogram viewer at `/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/`. The main work in this session was implementing full audio playback with: - Play/pause button with Space bar shortcut - Playhead (draggable vertical colored line in main canvas and overview) - Variable-rate playback (1/32× to 1×) using AudioWorklet + SharedArrayBuffer - Cmd+click on overview to seek; double-click on main canvas to seek - Call selection advances playhead - Numerous bug fixes and UI refinements The final set of requests (partially implemented): 1. Remove the "Pick" slider, set default pick distance to 10px 2. Widen the playback rate slider 3. Put "Position: HH:MM:SS.sss" text next to the rate slider in the header (to the right) 4. Replace "Zoom In"/"Zoom Out"/"Fit All" text buttons with icon buttons (⊕/⊖/⌂) and put them to the left of the call navigation buttons on one line 2. Key Technical Concepts: - AudioWorkletProcessor with variable-rate linear-interpolation resampler - SharedArrayBuffer (SAB) ring buffer: RING_SIZE=1,920,000 frames (10s at 192kHz); Int32[0..3] control header + Float32 data at byte 16 - Step formula: `step = (srcSr / sampleRate) * rate` — at 1/16×, srcSr=192kHz, ctxSr=48kHz → step=0.25, 40kHz bat call → 625Hz audible - Atomics for lock-free producer/consumer between worklet and worker - COOP/COEP HTTP headers required for SharedArrayBuffer (`Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin`, `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`) - Worklet `this._pos` is the only authoritative position; SAB ctrl[0] is written BY the worklet, not read by it — main thread must use `port.postMessage({type:'seek', frame})` to reposition - Fetch Web Worker prefetches chunks: CHUNK_FRAMES=192,000, TARGET_AHEAD=768,000; generation counter to cancel stale fetches on seek - Continuous logarithmic rate slider: `rate = 2^(v/100*5−5)`, snap points at v=0,20,40,60,80,100 - Magnetic snap: within ±3 slider units of a snap stop, the thumb jumps to that stop - `_waitForBuffer(startFrame)`: polls SAB ctrl[1] until 192,000 frames are prefetched before enabling ctrl[2]=1 to prevent initial-click underrun - Soft underrun fade: `this._gain *= 0.998` per sample (≈−10dB over 10ms) instead of hard zero-fill - Flask `np.memmap`-style serving: uses soundfile `seek/read` with `audio_lock`, serves raw float32 mono via `/api/audio_chunk` 3. Files and Code Sections: - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/audio-worklet.js`** - Variable-rate resampler AudioWorkletProcessor; SAB ring buffer reader - Added `this._last=0.0`, `this._gain=1.0`; `port.onmessage` seek handler; soft underrun fade ```javascript class BatPlayerProcessor extends AudioWorkletProcessor { constructor (options) { super(); const { sab, srcSr } = options.processorOptions; this._ctrl = new Int32Array(sab, 0, 4); this._data = new Float32Array(sab, 16, RING_SIZE); this._srcSr = srcSr; this._pos = 0.0; this._last = 0.0; this._gain = 1.0; this.port.onmessage = ({ data }) => { if (data.type === 'seek') { this._pos = data.frame; this._gain = 1.0; } }; } process (_inputs, outputs) { // ... if underrun: this._gain *= 0.998; ch0[i] = this._last * this._gain; // ... else: this._last = s; this._gain = 1.0; ch0[i] = s; } } ``` - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/audio-worker.js`** - Fetch Web Worker; prefetches chunks into SAB ring buffer - RING_SIZE=1,920,000; CHUNK_FRAMES=192,000; TARGET_AHEAD=768,000 - Generation counter (`_generation`) cancels stale fetch loops on seek/stop - `_seek(frame)` bumps generation, resets ctrl[0] and ctrl[1] to frame - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/player.js`** - Full playback engine; public API: `audioInit`, `audioPlayPause`, `audioSeek`, `audioSetRate`, `updatePlayButton` - Rate table: `_SNAP_VALUES=[0,20,40,60,80,100]`, `_SNAP_LABELS=['1/32×',...,'1×']`, `_SNAP_THRESH=3` - `_sliderToRate(v) = Math.pow(2, v/100*5-5)` - `_rateLabel(v)`: returns snap label or `×N.NN` - `_waitForBuffer(startFrame)`: polls 16ms until ctrl[1]-startFrame ≥ 192,000 or 2s timeout - `_playPending` guard prevents double-init during async buffer wait - `_audioPlay()`: lazy AudioContext creation, sends `{type:'seek',frame}` to both worklet port AND worker, waits for buffer, then ctrl[2]=1 - `audioSeek(t)`: when playing, pauses ctrl[2]=0, sends seek to worklet+worker, waits for buffer, resumes ctrl[2]=1 - RAF loop updates `S.playheadTime` from `Atomics.load(_ctrl, 0)` each frame; no viewport auto-scroll - `_initRateSlider()`: sets slider.value='20', magnetic snap on input - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/routes.py`** - Added `@app.after_request` COOP/COEP headers - Added `/api/audio_chunk?f=&frame=&n=` endpoint: reads via `entry.audio_fh.seek/read`, returns mono float32, capped at `sr` frames (192,000 at 192kHz), `Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy: same-origin` - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/state.js`** - Added `playheadTime: 0`, `isPlaying: false` - Changed `pickRadius: 20` → `pickRadius: 10` (most recent change) - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/render.js`** - `PLAYHEAD_COLOR = '#00d488'` - `drawPlayhead(W, H)`: vertical line + downward triangle handle at top, called between `drawRuler` and `drawCrosshairs` - `drawOverview()`: added playhead line + upward triangle handle at bottom (`_ovPhDrag` brightens it) - Time display now shows position via `fmtHMS(S.playheadTime)` (currently in `time-display` element, to be moved) - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/events.js`** - `PLAYHEAD_GRAB_PX=9`; `_phDrag` state; grab check in canvas mousedown after freq-axis check; move in window mousemove; finalize+seek in window mouseup - `_ovPhDrag` state; grab check at bottom 14px of overview (±10px of phOX); move in window mousemove; finalize+seek in window mouseup; cursor hint in ovCanvas mousemove - `dblclick` on main canvas → `audioSeek(xToT(mx))` - Space bar → `audioPlayPause()` (skipped when INPUT/TEXTAREA focused) - Cmd+click on overview → `audioSeek(ovXT(ox))` - `handleClick` advances `S.playheadTime = call.t0` and calls `audioSeek` if playing - `slider-pick-radius` oninput handler (still present, **needs to be removed**) - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/ui.js`** - Added `fmtHMS(t)`: formats seconds as `HH:MM:SS.sss` - `navigateCall` and `jumpToCallId` both set `S.playheadTime = call.t0` and call `audioSeek` if playing - `init()` calls `audioInit(S.fid, info.sr, Math.round(info.duration_s * info.sr))` and `_initRateSlider()` after info loads - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/app.css`** - Controls grid: `grid-template-columns: auto minmax(0,1fr) minmax(0,2fr) auto` (4 columns) - `#btn-play:hover { background: #133a2a !important; }` (old 5-rule block removed) - **`/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/templates/index.html`** - Header: added centered playback div with `flex:1; justify-content:center`: ```html
1/16×
``` - Classifier div: removed `margin-left:auto` (flex:1 on playback div handles centering) - Position ctrl-group: only contains label + time-display (playback controls moved to header) - Scripts: added `` before events.js - Zoom ctrl-group still has two separate rows (pending merge) - Pick slider still present in Contours group (pending removal from HTML) 4. Errors and Fixes: - **Worklet seek position ignored on play**: `this._pos` never updated from main thread; SAB ctrl[0] is written by worklet, not read. Fix: added `port.onmessage` seek handler in worklet constructor; main thread sends `_node.port.postMessage({type:'seek', frame})`. - **Click/pop at playback start**: ctrl[2]=1 set before worker fetched any data → immediate underrun. Fix: `_waitForBuffer(startFrame)` polls until 1s prefetched. Also increased CHUNK_FRAMES/TARGET_AHEAD. - **Hard underrun zero-fill click**: abrupt 0.12→0.0 transition. Fix: exponential decay `this._gain *= 0.998`. - **Controls crowding (5-column grid)**: User said "The play control is crowding the other controls." Fixed by merging into Position column, then later moved entirely to header. - **Viewport auto-scroll**: User said "The current view bounds should not snap to the playhead." Removed the auto-scroll block from `_startRAF`. - **Duplicate btn-zoom-in handler**: accidentally added an extra `document.getElementById('btn-zoom-in').onclick` line. Removed the duplicate. - **"Playback" label not wanted**: User said remove it. Removed `
Playback
` from HTML. 5. Problem Solving: - Logarithmic rate slider with magnetic snap: `rate = 2^(v/100×5−5)` maps 0→1/32×, 100→1×. Snap points at multiples of 20. Magnetic threshold ±3 units. On snap, `slider.value` is programmatically set so thumb visually jumps. - Overview playhead triangle: drawn at bottom of overview canvas (pointing up), grab zone is bottom 14px ± 10px of phOX. Uses `_ovPhDrag` state separate from `_ovDrag`. - Buffer prefetch race: `_playPending` flag prevents double-entry during the async `_waitForBuffer` call. 6. All User Messages: - "I don't think the full titles etc. of the species references are correct. Just use the abbreviated titles you put in the species info." - "Where it says 'Treat classification results ... with appropriate scepticism.' change this to 'Treat species information and classification results ... with intense scepticism.' Where it says 'It works' change it to 'The UI works well enough'\n\nWhere it says 'heuristics tuned to one recording' put 'heuristics tuned to only a few recordings'" - "I added a new wav file recorded at the Bracken Bat Cave in san antonio. Add it to the list. Maybe add location info in the status. Then start the server." - "That is not at all what I want. Undo that. Maybe put the # calls between the # tiles and timestamp." - "crop the 'bats on campbell' flac file to start at about 19:45 (i.e. 3 minutes into the file.) Crop the actual file. I'll remove the tiles/json and run it through the detection then." - "the second bracken file (....-back-bats.wav) doesn't have a location associated with it. It was also recorded at Bracken Bat Cave in San Antonio, TX" - "I'd like to experiment with audio playback in the browser..." (discussion of audio libraries) - "So there will be several HTTP requests each time it has so fetch a new segment? Is this the best way, or are there better ways to do streaming (websockets, streaming protocols?)" - "What would you do to avoid interruptions to playback from buffering" - "How about server-side. messages.text:
Done. Here's how it all fits together:

**While dragging (playing):**
- Every mousemove updates `S.playheadTime` immediately (visual is always live)
- `_scrubSeek()` resets a 200 ms debounce timer each move — only fires when the user slows/pauses
- When the timer fires it calls `audioSeek(t, scrub=true)` which uses a 0.25 s prebuffer instead of 1 s, so audio resumes faster
- Fast scrubbing = visual only, no audio chaos; slow/paused scrubbing = you hear the new position ~200–400 ms later

**Concurrent seek guard (`_seekInProgress` / `_seekQueued`):**
- If a scrub seek is already buffering and a new one arrives, the new target is stored in `_seekQueued`
- When the current seek finishes it checks the queue and tail-calls itself to the latest position — so you always land on where the user last paused, never a stale in-flight position

**On mouseup:**
- `clearTimeout(_scrubTimer)` cancels any pending scrub seek
- `audioSeek(S.playheadTime)` (no `scrub` flag) does a full 1 s prebuffer for a clean, click-free resume from the final position

messages.text: The worklet is the right place for this — it operates sample-by-sample and can apply a linear gain ramp at the output stage. The approach: track `_gain` (0→1) and ramp it toward the "target" (1 when playing+data available, 0 otherwise) by `1 / (sampleRate × 0.020)` per sample. That handles all three cases — start, stop, and underrun — with the same 20 ms linear fade, replacing the old exponential-decay hack.

messages.name: Write
messages.result: The file /Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/audio-worklet.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in y

messages.text:
Done. The old `this._gain = 1.0` initializer and `this._gain *= 0.998` exponential decay are both gone. Here's what the new system does in each case:

| Situation | What happens |
|---|---|
| **Play starts** | Seek resets `_gain=0`; when `ctrl[2]` → 1 and data arrives, gain ramps 0→1 over 20 ms |
| **Pause / stop** | `ctrl[2]` → 0; gain ramps 1→0 over 20 ms holding the last sample, then the fast-path silence fires |
| **Seek while playing** | Seek resets `_gain=0, _last=0`; audio is silent while buffering, then fades in cleanly |
| **Buffer underrun** | `hasData` becomes false mid-play; gain ramps down the same way — no abrupt cut |
| **Buffer recovers** | `hasData` goes true again; gain ramps back up to 1 |

The `FADE_STEP = 1 / (sampleRate × 0.020)` means exactly 960 samples (at 48 kHz) to traverse the full 0–1 range regardless of browser sample rate.
It seems to work when I play/pause, bt it doesn't work when there's a buffer underrun.

The remaining click on underrun recovery is a content discontinuity, not a gain discontinuity. Here's the problem: during underrun `pos` keeps advancing (to stay "in sync"), but `this._last` holds the sample from where data ran out. When data arrives, we jump to reading from wherever `pos` has gotten to — which is now different audio content than `this._last`. Even with gain=0.3 at the recovery point, `(this._last − new_sample) × 0.3` is a click.

The fix: **don't advance `pos` during underrun**. Keep the read cursor frozen at the last valid position. When the buffer refills, we continue from exactly where we left off — `ring[pos % RING_SIZE]` still contains the data we need (RING_SIZE is 10s so it won't have been overwritten), and the first recovered sample is numerically continuous with `this._last`. It also means the worker correctly keeps `ctrl[0]` frozen, so it knows to prefetch forward and the underrun resolves as soon as one chunk arrives.

Now the underrun path and the play/pause path are both clean for the same reason: `gain` always passes through 0 at the silence boundary, and the first non-silent sample is numerically close to what preceded it.

**The button** sits right after ▶ in the header. Its icon is a small centred playhead (triangle + vertical line) flanked by two viewport-edge markers — the "playhead locked to centre" concept at a glance. It's dim/grey when off and lights up in the same green as the play button when on.

**The scroll logic** (in `_startRAF`):
- Computes `centre = viewStart + viewDur / 2`
- If `playheadTime >= centre` → shifts `viewStart` so the playhead stays exactly at centre
- If `playheadTime < centre` → does nothing; the playhead advances naturally until it hits centre, then scrolling begins

messages.text: The server already caps at 4s and uses `scipy.signal.welch` — response time should be 50–100ms. The real bottleneck is the **client-side threshold**: at 1/16× playback, 0.05s of audio takes ~800ms real time, so the PSD only updates roughly once every 800ms. The fix is to switch from an audio-time threshold to a real-time rate limit.

messages.name: Edit
messages.result: The file /Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/render.js has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your con

messages.text:
The key changes:

- **Threshold: 0.05s → 0.0005s** — the old 50ms audio-time threshold was the root cause. At 1/16× speed the playhead moves ~1ms of audio per real-time frame, so the old threshold would only trigger a refetch every ~800ms. Now "any perceptible change" counts.

- **Real-time rate limit: 100ms** (`PSD_MIN_INTERVAL_MS`) — this replaces the old audio-time gating. It means at most ~10 PSD fetches per second regardless of playback rate, which is fast enough to feel live and cheap enough not to overload the server. If a fetch takes 150ms to come back, you get ~6fps; at 50ms you get 10fps.

- **Removed the 80ms debounce on the follow-up** — the old code did `setTimeout(fetchPSD, 80)` when a request was in flight. Now if `_psdPending=true` we simply return; the `finally` block calls `schedulePSDFetch()` (which itself respects the rate limit) as soon as the previous request finishes. No artificial extra wait.

- **`_psdLastFetchAt` tracks real time** so the rate-limit math is based on wall-clock milliseconds, not audio seconds.

messages.text:
Everything lines up. Here's what was done and why it eliminates the lag:

**Root cause:** In playhead mode, the app was making an HTTP round-trip to the server (`/api/psd`) for every update — even though all the audio data needed to compute the spectrum was already sitting in the `SharedArrayBuffer` ring buffer that the audio worker pre-fills.

**What's new:**

**`player.js`**
- `_ringData = new Float32Array(_sab, 16, _RING_SIZE)` — a cached typed-array view of the ring buffer's audio data, created once when the AudioContext initialises
- `audioGetFrames(startFrame, count)` — copies a contiguous slice out of the ring, handling wraparound, with bounds checks against the write cursor (`_ctrl[1]`)
- `audioSrcSr()` — exposes `_srcSr` so render.js doesn't have to guess it

**`render.js`**
- Pre-computed Hann window (`_lHann`) and persistent FFT scratch buffers (`_lRe`/`_lIm`) allocated once at load — no GC pressure at 20 fps
- `_lfft()` — in-place radix-2 Cooley-Tukey FFT, 1024 points
- `_tryLocalPSD()` — extracts ±100ms of audio around the playhead, runs a Welch average (same nperseg=1024/noverlap=768 as the server), produces the same `{freqs, powers, vmin, vmax}` format that `drawPSD()` already consumes. Rate-limited to 50ms (20 fps); returns `true` even when throttled so the server fetch is suppressed.
- `schedulePSDFetch()` — now checks `S.psdMode === 'playhead' && _tryLocalPSD()` first; only falls through to the server fetch when not playing or when the ring buffer is unavailable (e.g. just after first load).

The FFT computation itself costs roughly 2–5ms per call (147 × 1024-pt FFTs via Welch), so at 20 fps that's ~4–10% of a CPU core — well within budget for a background visualisation.

messages.text:
Deployed. A few things to note about what happened and what to expect:

**What was wrong:** The COOP/COEP headers are correct (`curl` confirmed them). This is a browser-specific issue — `Can't find variable: SharedArrayBuffer` is Safari's error, not Chrome's. Safari has a stricter implementation: even with the right headers, there are edge cases where cross-origin isolation doesn't engage (certain iOS versions, WKWebView in-app browsers, or cached page states from before the headers were added). The underlying audio system was silently crashing as an unhandled promise rejection every time you pressed play.

**What's fixed:** The crash is now caught. If `SharedArrayBuffer` is unavailable, pressing play will show a message in the status bar — *"Audio unavailable: browser requires a secure cross-origin context. Try reloading, or use Chrome/Firefox."* — instead of a silent failure in the console. The rest of the UI (spectrogram, calls, PSD via server) continues to work normally.

**If you're on Safari and audio still doesn't work after reloading:** try a hard reload (Cmd+Shift+R) to flush any cached version of the page from before the COOP/COEP headers were deployed. If it still fails, Chrome or Firefox on the same machine will definitely work.

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's what will happen on the next page load:

1. **First visit** — `crossOriginIsolated` is false, the service worker registers, activates, and the page reloads once automatically (you'll see a quick flash)
2. **All subsequent visits** — the SW is already active, every response gets COOP + COEP + CORP headers injected at the browser level before the page even sees them; `crossOriginIsolated` is true from the first frame, SharedArrayBuffer is available

The `sessionStorage` flag means if the reload somehow doesn't fix it (e.g. the SW failed to activate), it won't loop — it'll just give up and let you see whatever error remains.

If after the reload you still see the SharedArrayBuffer error in Chrome, run `window.crossOriginIsolated` in the DevTools console and tell me what it returns — that'll tell us whether the isolation is actually taking effect.

window.crossOriginIsolated
false
[Request interrupted by user]

?f=d789a820&t=0.0&vd…0&fl=13.0&fh=96.0:1 The Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header has been ignored, because the URL's origin was untrustworthy. It was defined either in the final response or a redirect. Please deliver the response using the HTTPS protocol. You can also use the 'localhost' origin instead. See https://www.w3.org/TR/powerful-features/#potentially-trustworthy-origin and https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#the-cross-origin-opener-policy-header.

player.js:191 Audio init failed: Error: SharedArrayBuffer not available — page must be cross-origin-isolated (COOP + COEP headers).
    at _initContext (player.js:205:11)
    at _audioPlay (player.js:167:13)
    at audioPlayPause (player.js:65:11)
    at HTMLButtonElement.onclick (?f=d789a820&t=0.0&vd…13.0&fh=96.0:17:187)

window.crossOriginIsolated
false
[Request interrupted by user]

The smoking gun is right there in the error:

> **`The Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header has been ignored, because the URL's origin was untrustworthy … Please deliver the response using the HTTPS protocol.`**

The browser is loading the app over **HTTP** (`http://bats.dedyn.io`), not HTTPS. COOP/COEP are silently ignored over plain HTTP — the browser won't enter cross-origin-isolated mode no matter what the headers say. The nginx config has both port 80 and 443 in the same server block with no HTTP→HTTPS redirect, so port 80 traffic just proxies straight to Flask.

The fix: add a `before_request` redirect in Flask. The nginx config already forwards `X-Forwarded-Proto: http/https`, so we can use that to redirect without affecting local development (which has no proxy, so the header is never set).

Now I have the full picture. Let me answer clearly and then fix the real problem.

**The parameters** — local FFT uses `nperseg=1024`, `step=256`, symmetric Hann window — they're identical to the server's `/api/psd`. That's not the issue.

**The actual problem**: the analysis window is ±100ms = 200ms of source audio. A bat call lasts 5–20ms, so it only occupies ~3–10% of the Welch average. The other 90–97% is background noise, which has a genuine 1/f-like shape even in the 13–96 kHz range — more power at lower frequencies, less at higher. The per-window normalization (vmin/vmax from the window itself) maps this sloping noise floor to fill the full display range, showing exactly the "offset that gradually decreases at higher frequencies" you're seeing.

The server's `/api/psd` uses **global** `vmin`/`vmax` (the recording's overall noise floor and peak, computed from all tiles) so the noise floor always normalises near zero. Two fixes needed:

1. **Use the server's cached global `vmin`/`vmax`** for local normalization so the noise floor stays at the bottom
2. **Shorten the analysis window** from ±100ms to ±25ms — the bat call then represents a larger fraction of the average, making it more prominent

messages.text:
Deployed. To summarize the two problems and their fixes:

**Root cause 1 — sloping baseline**: The Welch average over ±100 ms was dominated by background noise (a 10 ms bat call is only ~5% of the window). Even after restricting normalization to the 13–96 kHz display range, the 1/f noise floor that slopes naturally across that range still filled the full [0,1] display range — hence "offset that decreases at higher frequencies."

**Fix 1 — global normalization**: The server's `vmin`/`vmax` are file-wide robust percentile statistics (median noise floor / 75th-percentile peak). Caching those from the first server PSD response and using them for local normalization means the noise floor always maps near 0, just as it does in the tile spectrogram. `_cachedGlobalVmin/Vmax` live in `state.js` and get populated every time a server PSD arrives.

**Root cause 2 — short calls diluted**: Even with correct normalization, a 10 ms call in a 200 ms window only contributes 5% to the average.

**Fix 2 — shorter window**: ±25 ms (50 ms total). The same call is now 20% of the average — 4× more prominent — while still using enough segments (~15) for reasonable smoothing.

One caveat: the global stats aren't available until the first server PSD fetch completes (which happens automatically when the page loads in view mode). If the user starts playing before that, the fallback per-window normalization is used briefly.

You're right, I've been overcomplicating this. The real issue was a floor mismatch: the server uses `+1e-12` in its dB conversion (`-120 dB` floor), while I used `1e-20` (`-200 dB` floor). When global `vmin`/`vmax` (calibrated to the server's `−120 dB` floor) get applied to my locally-computed `dBs` that can reach `−200 dB`, the normalisation completely breaks.

Additionally, "the FFT window should match the spectrogram" means **one 1024-sample FFT centered on the playhead** — no Welch averaging loop at all. Each spectrogram column is one such FFT. The playhead PSD should be exactly that.

messages.text:
Here's the precise answer to both questions:

**1. Window function — yes, Hann is applied**

The local PSD does apply a Hann window, exactly like the server:
```js
// _lHann[i] = 0.5 * (1 − cos(2π·i / (N−1)))   — symmetric, same as scipy
for (let i = 0; i < _L_NPERSEG; i++) _lRe[i] = samples[i] * _lHann[i];
```
That matches scipy's `scipy.signal.get_window('hann', 1024)` which also uses `N−1` in the denominator. So the window is not the problem.

**2. Sample rate — full source rate, no downsampling**

`audioGetFrames()` reads directly from the SharedArrayBuffer ring buffer, which is written by the prefetch worker at the original source rate (`_srcSr = 192000` Hz from `/api/info`). The AudioWorklet resamples *for playback* (ring buffer → AudioContext rate), but that path is independent. The PSD reads from the same raw ring buffer, so it's operating at 192 kHz. That's also correct.

**Why it's noisy — variance, not error**

The noisiness is completely expected: a single periodogram (one FFT realization) has variance proportional to the signal power squared — it never converges no matter how long you record. The HTTP Welch PSD averages over all overlapping 1024-sample windows in the visible time range (e.g. a 30 s window at 192 kHz gives ~22,000 FFT windows). Variance drops as 1/K where K = number of windows averaged, so the server curve is ~100× smoother.

One 1024-sample FFT at 192 kHz spans only **5.3 ms** — it IS exactly one spectrogram column, which is why the spectrogram itself looks speckled rather than smooth.

**The fix:** A small Welch average centered on the playhead. E.g., ±25 ms (5 × 1024 / 192000 s ≈ 9 FFT windows with 75% overlap) keeps it "instantaneous" (bat calls are 5–20 ms) while dropping variance ~10×. That's what the prior version was doing — the shape was wrong there because of the `1e-20` floor and sub-display-bin contamination, not because of averaging. Want me to restore a short Welch window (~±10–25 ms) with the corrected floor and normalization?

Found it. Line 1070: in playhead mode the client sends `t0 = playheadTime − 0.1`, `t1 = playheadTime + 0.1` — a **200 ms window**. The server runs `scipy.signal.spectrogram` (Welch) over that and averages all the columns: `Sxx[bm, :].mean(axis=1)`. At 192 kHz that's ~147 overlapping FFTs averaged together — very smooth.

The HTTP playhead PSD is definitely **not** a single FFT. It's a 200 ms Welch average centered on the playhead, capped at 4 s. So the local PSD needs to match that: Welch over ±100 ms, same `nperseg=1024`, `noverlap=768`, `+1e-12` floor, same normalization. Let me fix it.

messages.result: [main 946f7a1] Fix local PSD: Welch-average ±100 ms to match server playhead window

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's what was wrong and what changed:

The HTTP playhead PSD was **never** a single FFT — it always was a Welch average over a **200 ms window** (±100 ms around the playhead). At 192 kHz that's ~38,400 samples → ~147 overlapping FFTs averaged together, which is why it was so much smoother.

The local path now does exactly the same thing:
- Reads `playheadTime ± 100 ms` from the ring buffer  
- Runs the same Welch loop (`nperseg=1024`, `noverlap=768`, step=256)  
- `+1e-12` floor, same normalization

The only remaining difference between local and HTTP will be the tiny latency of what's in the ring buffer vs what's on disk — they should otherwise produce nearly identical curves.

There seems to be some sort of delay again. Where I'll hear a chirp, the PSD will react about half a second later, and then sort of hold its shape even though the chirp is gone. While I'd expect some sort of temporal smoothing from what you describe, it seems to be much more than I see in the spectrogram. Are you absolutely sure you know what the spectrogram is doing?

messages.text:
Now I see the problem clearly. The window is `±100 ms source time`, but at 1/16× playback rate, 100 ms source = **1.6 seconds wall-clock**. So the 200 ms source window smears over 3.2 seconds of perceived time — that's why the PSD "holds" the chirp long after it's gone.

And the "half a second later" reaction: the chirp's energy only dominates the Welch average when it's near the center of the window; at 1/16×, that convergence happens ~0.5 s wall-clock after first entering the window.

The fix: **scale the window by the playback rate** so it always represents ~200 ms wall-clock time regardless of rate. I need to add a rate getter to player.js and use it in `_tryLocalPSD`.

Fix local PSD temporal lag: rate-adaptive Welch window

The Welch window was fixed at ±100 ms *source* time.  At slow playback
rates (e.g. 1/16×) that maps to ±1.6 s wall-clock, making the PSD
appear to lag the chirp by ~0.5 s and hold the shape for several seconds
after the chirp passes.

Scale the window by the current playback rate so it always spans
~±100 ms wall-clock.  At 1/16× this is ±6.25 ms source; at 1× it is
the full ±100 ms that matches the HTTP /api/psd endpoint.  A minimum
of 2048 samples (≈5 FFT windows) is enforced to keep the curve smooth
even at the slowest rates.

Add audioPlaybackRate() to player.js to expose _ctrl[3] to render.js.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main && git push piku main
messages.result: [main 69e2f9b] Fix local PSD temporal lag: rate-adaptive Welch window

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's what was wrong and what changed:

**Root cause**: the window was `±100 ms source time`, hardcoded. At 1/16× playback, 100 ms source = 1.6 s wall-clock — so the chirp was smeared over a 3.2 s perceived window, and you wouldn't see it dominate the average until it was well-centered (~0.5 s wall-clock after first entering the window).

**Fix**: the window now scales with playback rate so it always represents **±100 ms wall-clock**:
- `halfN = max(2048, round(0.1 × rate × srcSr))`
- At **1/16×**: halfN = 2048 samples = 10.7 ms source → **~170 ms wall-clock** total window
- At **1/8×**: ~25 ms source → **~200 ms wall-clock**
- At **1×**: 19200 samples = 100 ms source → **200 ms wall-clock** (same as HTTP endpoint)

The minimum of 2048 samples ensures ~5 FFT windows worth of averaging at the slowest rates so the curve stays reasonably smooth. The PSD should now track chirps with the same perceived responsiveness at all playback speeds.

That doesn't seem right. Now it's much noisier again.

The spectrum should always be computed the same way using the same window size in samples regardless of the playback rate. Because the spectrogram isn't recomputed depending on the playback rate. I'll say it again: the realtime spectrum should exactly equal a single frame of the pre-computed spectrogram.

Is the slowed down playback being resampled, or just playing the samples lower? The FFT frames should be computed on the incoming samples from the HTTP request before any sort of processing is done on them, perhaps.

You're absolutely right, and I should have held to that. The spectrogram is fixed at source rate regardless of playback — one 1024-sample Hann-windowed FFT per column. The realtime PSD should be identical: one such FFT from the ring buffer at the playhead position.

On your resampling question: yes, the AudioWorklet does resample — it steps through the ring buffer at `(srcSr / audioContextSampleRate) × rate` samples per output sample (linear interpolation). But `audioGetFrames` reads the raw pre-resampling ring buffer directly, so the FFT is already computed on the original 192 kHz source samples. That's exactly right.

To be clear on what that means visually: the PSD will be as "noisy" as a single column of the spectrogram looks, which is inherent to a single periodogram. That's the correct tradeoff if the goal is temporal accuracy matching the spectrogram. If later you want it smoother without adding lag, the right approach would be exponential averaging across successive frames (IIR smoothing in time) rather than a larger window — that averages the curve as it scrolls without any forward/backward temporal smear.

messages.detail:
cd /Users/brandon/claude/xpabat && git add config.py registry.py tiles.py routes.py static/state.js static/render.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
PSD: fixed 1%/99% dB scale with monotonic expansion

Server:
- Compute psd_p01 / psd_p99 (true 1st/99th-percentile dB across all
  display-range bins, subsampled from up to 30 tiles) in tile norm pass.
- Store in norm.json; load with fallback to vmin/vmax for old norms.
- Bump TILE_NORM_VERSION to 10 to trigger regeneration.
- /api/psd now returns raw dbs[] + psd_p01/psd_p99 instead of
  pre-normalised powers; client owns all normalisation.

Client:
- _psdScaleMin / _psdScaleMax replace _cachedGlobalVmin/Max.
- Initialised from psd_p01/psd_p99 on first /api/psd response.
- Both local (ring-buffer) and HTTP PSD paths expand the scale when a
  frame's dBs exceed the current bounds; the scale never shrinks.
- Bootstrap from frame's own dB range if server stats not yet available.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main && git push piku main
messages.result: [main d555125] PSD: fixed 1%/99% dB scale with monotonic expansion

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's what changed across the stack:

**Server** (`tiles.py`): during the 30-tile norm pass, every 16th spectrogram column's dB values are pooled and the true 1st/99th percentile is computed across all of them — a proper file-wide sample. Stored as `psd_p01`/`psd_p99` in `norm.json`. Version bumped to 10 so existing norms regenerate (old files fall back to `vmin`/`vmax` until they do).

**`/api/psd`**: now returns raw `dbs[]` + `psd_p01`/`psd_p99` instead of pre-normalised `powers`. The client owns all normalisation.

**Client** (`_psdScaleMin`/`_psdScaleMax`):
- First `/api/psd` response initialises the scale from `psd_p01`/`psd_p99`
- Every subsequent frame (HTTP or ring-buffer) only expands the range if the dBs fall outside it — never shrinks
- If playing starts before any HTTP fetch, the scale bootstraps from the first ring-buffer frame's display-range dBs

from PIL import Image, ImageDraw, ImageFont
import io, struct

sizes = [16, 32, 48]
frames = []

for sz in sizes:
    img = Image.new("RGBA", (sz, sz), (0, 0, 0, 0))
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)
    # Try to use a system font that supports emoji
    font = None
    font_size = int(sz * 0.85)
    for path in [
        "/System/Library/Fonts/Apple Color Emoji.ttc",
        "/System/Library/Fonts/Supplemental/Apple Color Emoji.ttc",

            font = ImageFont.truetype(path, font_size)

    frames.append(img)

frames[0].save(
    "static/favicon.ico",
    format="ICO",
    sizes=[(s, s) for s in sizes],
    append_images=frames[1:],
)
print("Done:", [f.size for f in frames])

messages.detail:
python3 - <<'EOF'
# Use PIL to draw the bat emoji using the color emoji font properly
# Apple Color Emoji requires PILFONT-level tricks; let's try a different approach:
# render each size as a proper RGBA PNG, then pack into ICO manually

from PIL import Image, ImageDraw, ImageFont
import struct, io

def make_frame(sz):
    # Create a larger surface, draw, then downsample for better quality
    scale = 2
    big = sz * scale
    img = Image.new("RGBA", (big, big), (0, 0, 0, 0))
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)
    font_size = int(big * 0.80)
    font = None
    for path in [
        "/System/Library/Fonts/Apple Color Emoji.ttc",
    ]:
        try:
            font = ImageFont.truetype(path, font_size)
            break
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Font load error: {e}")
    if font is None:
        font = ImageFont.load_default()
    
    # Draw with embedded_color for emoji
    bbox = draw.textbbox((0, 0), "🦇", font=font, embedded_color=True)
    x = (big - (bbox[2] - bbox[0])) // 2 - bbox[0]
    y = (big - (bbox[3] - bbox[1])) // 2 - bbox[1]
    draw.text((x, y), "🦇", font=font, embedded_color=True)
    
    # Downscale with LANCZOS
    out = img.resize((sz, sz), Image.LANCZOS)
    non_t = sum(1 for p in list(out.convert("RGBA").getdata()) if p[3] > 0)
    print(f"  {sz}x{sz}: {non_t}/{sz*sz} non-transparent px")
    return out

sizes = [16, 32, 48]
frames = []
for sz in sizes:
    frames.append(make_frame(sz))

# Save as ICO
frames[0].save("static/favicon.ico", format="ICO",
               sizes=[(s, s) for s in sizes],
               append_images=frames[1:])

import os
print("ICO size:", os.path.getsize("static/favicon.ico"))
EOF

python3 - <<'EOF'
# Try NSImage on macOS to render the emoji
import subprocess, sys, os

script = """
import Cocoa, struct, zlib, io

def make_png(sz):
    img = Cocoa.NSImage.alloc().initWithSize_((sz, sz))
    img.lockFocus()
    attrs = {
        Cocoa.NSFontAttributeName: Cocoa.NSFont.fontWithName_size_("Apple Color Emoji", sz * 0.8),
    }
    s = "🦇"
    nsstr = Cocoa.NSString.stringWithString_(s)
    bstr = Cocoa.NSAttributedString.alloc().initWithString_attributes_(nsstr, attrs)
    sz2 = bstr.size()
    x = (sz - sz2.width) / 2
    y = (sz - sz2.height) / 2
    bstr.drawAtPoint_((x, y))
    img.unlockFocus()
    
    rep = img.representations()[0]
    png_data = rep.representationUsingType_properties_(Cocoa.NSBitmapImageFileTypePNG, None)
    return bytes(png_data)

from PIL import Image
import io as _io
sizes = [16, 32, 48]
frames = []
for sz in sizes:
    png = make_png(sz)
    fr = Image.open(_io.BytesIO(png)).convert("RGBA")
    non_t = sum(1 for p in list(fr.getdata()) if p[3] > 0)
    print(f"{sz}x{sz}: {non_t}/{sz*sz} non-transparent px")
    frames.append(fr)

frames[0].save("static/favicon.ico", format="ICO",
               sizes=[(s, s) for s in [16, 32, 48]],
               append_images=frames[1:])

import os as _os
print("ICO size:", _os.path.getsize("static/favicon.ico"))
"""
result = subprocess.run([sys.executable, "-c", script], capture_output=True, text=True)
print(result.stdout)
if result.stderr:
    print("STDERR:", result.stderr[:500])

from PIL import Image

# Check each size
for sz in [16, 32, 48, 64]:
    img = Image.open(f"/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/favicon_{sz}.png").convert("RGBA")

# Build the ICO with actual pixel-correct sizes
sizes = [16, 32, 48]
frames = []
for sz in sizes:
    img = Image.open(f"/Users/brandon/claude/xpabat/static/favicon_{sz}.png").convert("RGBA")
    if img.size != (sz, sz):
        img = img.resize((sz, sz), Image.LANCZOS)
    frames.append(img)

frames[0].save(
    "static/favicon.ico",
    format="ICO",
    sizes=[(s, s) for s in sizes],
    append_images=frames[1:],
)

import os
print(f"\nfavicon.ico: {os.path.getsize('static/favicon.ico')} bytes")
EOF

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's the full picture of what was implemented:

**Loop behaviour:**
- When a ruler selection is fixed (drag released), `S.rulerLoopT0` / `S.rulerLoopT1` are stored as absolute times so the loop bounds are stable even if you pan afterward
- In the RAF `tick()`: when the worklet read position reaches `rulerLoopT1`, `audioSeek(rulerLoopT0)` is called — `_seekInProgress` prevents the call from firing again on every frame while the seek is in-flight
- When the ruler is dismissed (Escape, tiny-drag, or "Zoom to selection"), the loop times are cleared

**Auto-scroll suppression:**
- The `followPlayhead` scroll logic is gated on `!hasMarquee`, so with a selection active the view stays fixed — the playhead bounces within the visible box rather than dragging the viewport

6. Apply a high pass filter on audio when playing back at a slower rate to reduce the power in bass frequencies. This could be, for example, the same 13KHz cutoff that is used in analysis, but perhaps increasing from no to full attenuation between 1x and 1/8x.
Let me read the full `_initContext` and teardown to know exactly what to change:

The math: output cutoff = `13kHz × rate × ramp`, where `ramp` goes linearly from 0 (at rate=1) to 1 (at rate≤1/8). So at 1/16x the filter sits at 812 Hz; at 1x it's near 0 Hz (transparent). The filter node lives between the worklet and destination permanently.

messages.detail:
cd /Users/brandon/claude/xpabat && git add static/player.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add rate-adaptive high-pass filter to audio playback

Inserts a BiquadFilterNode (Butterworth highpass) between the AudioWorklet
and the output destination.  The cutoff frequency tracks the bat-call floor
(13 kHz) shifted into the output domain by the playback rate, and ramps in
linearly from transparent at 1× to full strength at ≤ 1/8×:

  ramp   = clamp((1 − rate) / (1 − 1/8), 0, 1)
  cutoff = ramp × 13 kHz × rate   (Hz in the output domain)

At 1/16× this yields ≈ 812 Hz; at 1/4× ≈ 2.8 kHz; at 1× ≈ 0 Hz (off).
Frequency transitions use setTargetAtTime (30 ms) so slider moves are smooth.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push origin main && git push piku main
messages.result: [main 5ad4502] Add rate-adaptive high-pass filter to audio playback

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's what was added — just 28 lines in `player.js`:

**Audio graph**: `worklet → BiquadFilter(highpass, Q=0.7071) → destination`. The filter is always in the chain but fully transparent at 1×.

**`_updateHPF()`** — called from `audioSetRate()` and on context init:
```
ramp   = clamp((1 − rate) / 0.875, 0, 1)   ← 0 at 1×, 1 at ≤ 1/8×
cutoff = ramp × 13 000 × rate               ← Hz in the output domain
```

| Rate | Ramp | Cutoff |
|------|------|--------|
| 1× | 0 | ≈1 Hz (off) |
| 1/4× | 0.86 | ≈2.8 kHz |
| 1/8× | 1.0 | 1625 Hz |
| 1/16× | 1.0 | 812 Hz |
| 1/32× | 1.0 | 406 Hz |

`setTargetAtTime` with a 30 ms time constant smooths the transitions so moving the rate slider doesn't produce clicks or abrupt tonal jumps.

Found it. The core bug: the rendering loop groups an assistant turn + all following tool turns into a single `parts` entry, so `parts.length < deduped.length`. - **Draw a marquee box** on the spectrogram → when you release, the frequency range (in kHz) is stored in `S.rulerLoopF0/F1`

- **Bypass**: when Esc / zoom-to-selection / tiny drag clears the marquee, both filters fade back to 1 Hz / Nyquist immediately (effectively transparent)

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's how it works:

**UI**: When you fix a marquee selection, a small "Band attenuation" slider appears just below the "⊕ Zoom to selection" button. It tracks the ruler info box position and disappears when the marquee is cleared.

**Audio math**: Rather than the BPF simply blocking or passing, the signal now splits into two parallel paths after the HPF:
- **Dry path** (`1 − attenuation`) — unfiltered, carries all frequencies  
- **Wet path** (attenuation) — goes through the low-cut + high-cut pair, carries only the selected band

Mixed together: in-band frequencies always arrive at full amplitude (`1 − att + att = 1`), while out-of-band is attenuated by `1 − att`. At 0% the bandpass is completely transparent; at 100% it's the full cut (previous behavior).

messages.text:
Now I understand the full picture. The gap comes from three things in `audioSeek`:
1. `Atomics.store(_ctrl, 2, 0)` — pauses the worklet
2. `_waitForBuffer` — waits up to 2 s for the worker to re-fetch
3. Worklet resets `_gain = 0` on `seek`, so there's a 20 ms fade-in even after data arrives

The fix: since the ring holds the last 10 s of audio, the loop-start data is **already in the ring** as long as the loop is ≤ 10 s. For a loop seek we can skip the pause, skip the buffer wait, and skip the gain reset. I'll add a `loop_seek` message type to both the worklet and worker, and a synchronous `_audioLoopSeek()` in player.js.

cd /Users/brandon/claude/xpabat && git add static/audio-worklet.js static/audio-worker.js static/player.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix marquee loop gap: seamless ring-buffer seek instead of pause+refetch

The previous loop used audioSeek() which pauses the worklet, resets the
ring write pointer to the loop start, and waits up to 2 s for the worker
to re-fetch data before resuming.  This caused a noticeable gap every loop.

Fix: the ring buffer holds the last 10 s of audio, so loop-start data is
already present as long as the loop is ≤ 10 s.  A new _audioLoopSeek()
path exploits this:

  • Sends 'loop_seek' to the worklet — resets _pos only, keeps _gain at
    its current value (1.0) so there is no fade-in gap at the loop point.
  • Sends 'loop_seek' to the worker — restarts the fetch loop from the
    new frame without touching ctrl[0]/ctrl[1], so the worklet never sees
    a write-pointer reset that would look like an underrun.
  • Never touches ctrl[2] (play state) — audio plays continuously.

Falls back to audioSeek() if the frame has been evicted from the ring
(first play-from-pause or loop > 10 s).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

messages.name: Bash
messages.detail: git push origin main && git push piku main 2>&1 | tail -5

messages.text:
Deployed. Here's what changed and why it's now seamless:

**Root cause**: `audioSeek` always (1) pauses the worklet, (2) resets both the ring read *and* write pointers to the seek target, then (3) waits up to 2 s for the worker to re-fetch data before resuming. All three steps contribute to the gap.

**Fix**: The ring buffer holds the last 10 s of audio. By the time the playhead reaches `loopT1`, the data at `loopT0` is already sitting in the ring (as long as the loop is ≤ 10 s). `_audioLoopSeek` exploits this:

- Sends `loop_seek` to the **worklet** — resets `_pos` to the loop start but leaves `_gain` at its current value (1.0), so there's no 20 ms fade-in ramp
- Sends `loop_seek` to the **worker** — restarts its fetch loop from the new position but *doesn't touch* `ctrl[1]` (write pointer), so the worklet never sees the write pointer jump backwards and trigger an underrun
- Never touches `ctrl[2]` (play flag) — the worklet stays in its playing state throughout

The only audible artifact would be a very brief interpolation discontinuity as `_pos` jumps, but since it's the same audio content the sample values should be continuous at the loop boundary.

It's continuous now, but it still clicks when it loops.

messages.text:
Deployed. Three changes:

**Loop click** — When `loop_seek` arrives, the worklet now saves `_pos` as `_xfadePos` and counts down `_xfadeN = 256`. For those 256 output frames (~5 ms), each sample blends linearly from the old loop-end position to the new loop-start position (`t` sweeps 0→1). This smooths any waveform step at the boundary without introducing silence or a gain ramp.

**Slider overflow** — Added `min-width: 0` to the range input inside `#bpf-att`, which allows it to shrink inside its flex container. The global `input[type=range]` rule doesn't have this, causing overflow.

**Slider style** — Scoped new styles to `#bpf-att input[type=range]` that override the global round-thumb rules: a 10 px tall filled rectangle, translucent orange fill up to the thumb, a 3 px hairline cursor for the thumb (no circle). The label and value text use the same dim-orange palette as the ruler info box.
Maybe instead of "band attenuation" make it say "filter strength"? Does this make sense? Is there a better term that is both technically accurate and user friendly?

"Filter strength" works and is intuitive — higher = more filtering. A few alternatives worth considering:

- **"Band isolation"** — probably the most precise: at 100% you hear *only* the selected band, at 0% the selection has no effect. It names what's happening, not the mechanism.
- **"Isolation"** alone — very short, and in context (after drawing a frequency band) it's unambiguous.
- **"Selectivity"** — used in radio/acoustics for exactly this concept, but less familiar to most people.
- **"Filter depth"** — common in audio plugin UIs, similar feel to "filter strength."

My pick would be **"Band isolation"** — it's technically accurate (you're isolating a frequency band), directly describes what the slider does at both ends (0 = no isolation, 100 = full isolation), and reads naturally to someone who isn't a signal processing expert. It also pairs well with the "marquee selection" context — you've drawn a band, now you control how isolated it is.

Want me to change it to that, or do you prefer one of the others?
filter strength is good

The pop is from `_gain = 0.0` being set **instantly** when the seek port message arrives, while the worklet's gain hasn't faded down yet. cd /Users/brandon/claude/xpabat && git add static/audio-worklet.js && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix playhead seek pop: don't reset _gain on seek message

The worklet's seek message handler was setting _gain = 0.0 immediately,
causing an instantaneous gain jump from whatever value the 20 ms fade-out
had reached → audible click.

The pause (ctrl[2]=0) that the caller always sets before sending 'seek'
already triggers the worklet's FADE_STEP ramp to silence.  By the time
the buffer is ready and ctrl[2] is restored, the ramp has had ≥20 ms to
complete (buffer wait is always longer), so _gain is naturally at 0 for
a clean fade-in.  Removing the explicit reset eliminates the click.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

messages.text:
Deployed. The sequence was:

1. `ctrl[2] = 0` — pause flag set synchronously, worklet immediately starts its 20 ms gain ramp to silence
2. Port message `{type:'seek', frame}` arrives a fraction of a millisecond later — gain was still near 1.0, so the old `_gain = 0.0` assignment caused the click
3. `_waitForBuffer` takes ≥ 50–250 ms — more than enough time for the 20 ms fade to complete naturally
4. `ctrl[2] = 1` — gain is already at 0, resumes with a clean fade-in

Removing the explicit `_gain = 0.0` assignment lets step 1's ramp handle the transition smoothly. The buffer wait guarantees the ramp is done long before playback resumes.

Key Technical Concepts: - Canvas 2D rendering for keyboard legend, ruler/marquee overlays, PSD sidebar - Web Audio API: BiquadFilterNode (HPF + BPF pair), GainNode (dry/wet mix), AudioWorklet, SharedArrayBuffer ring buffer - Ring-buffer seamless loop seek: `loop_seek` message resets `_pos` without pausing worklet or resetting ctrl[1] - Crossfade at loop boundary: 256-sample linear blend from old position to new position in worklet - Gain ramp protocol: ctrl[2]=0 (pause) triggers 20ms FADE_STEP ramp; seek message should NOT reset _gain - Dry/wet parallel filter mix: in-band always at full amplitude, out-of-band attenuated by slider value - DOM overlay positioning: `getBoundingClientRect()` for scroll targeting; canvas-space coordinates for absolute-positioned overlays - Section nav scrollspy: `getBoundingClientRect()` relative to modal body container - deduped[] vs parts[] index divergence: tool entries folded into assistant bubble = fewer parts than deduped entries 3. Files and Code Sections: - **`static/audio-worklet.js`** — Multiple changes: - Added `_xfadePos`, `_xfadeN` fields for crossfade - `loop_seek` message: resets `_pos` only, saves old pos for crossfade, keeps `_gain` unchanged - `seek` message: now only resets `_pos` and cancels crossfade (`_xfadeN = 0`); does NOT reset `_gain` (pause ramp handles fade-out cleanly) - Crossfade blend in process() loop: when `_xfadeN > 0`, linearly blends old→new sample over 256 output frames ```javascript this.port.onmessage = ({ data }) => { if (data.type === 'seek') { this._pos = data.frame; this._xfadeN = 0; // _gain intentionally NOT reset — pause ramp handles fade-out cleanly } else if (data.type === 'loop_seek') { this._xfadePos = this._pos; this._xfadeN = 256; this._pos = data.frame; } }; // In process(): if (this._xfadeN > 0) { const xf0 = Math.floor(this._xfadePos); const xfrc = this._xfadePos - xf0; const xi0 = xf0 % RING_SIZE; const xi1 = (xf0 + 1) % RING_SIZE; const sOld = this._data[xi0] + xfrc * (this._data[xi1] - this._data[xi0]); const t = 1 - this._xfadeN / 256; s = s * t + sOld * (1 - t); this._xfadePos += step; this._xfadeN--; } ``` - **`static/audio-worker.js`** — Added `loop_seek` message type: ```javascript } else if (msg.type === 'loop_seek') { _generation++; _writeFrame = Math.max(0, Math.min(_totalFrames, msg.frame)); _loop(_generation); // Does NOT touch ctrl[0] or ctrl[1] — ring already has valid data } ``` - **`static/player.js`** — Multiple changes: - Added `_bpfLo`, `_bpfHi`, `_dryGain`, `_wetGain`, `_bpfAtt = 1` - Filter chain (dry/wet parallel): ``` worklet → HPF → _dryGain (gain = 1-att) → destination ↘ _bpfLo → _bpfHi → _wetGain (gain = att) → destination ``` - `_updateBPF()`: sets BPF cutoffs = `rulerLoopF0/F1 * 1000 * rate` Hz when marquee active; sets dry/wet gains; bypasses (1 Hz / Nyquist) when no marquee - `audioUpdateBPF()`: public, called from events.js - `audioSetBPFAtt(att)`: sets `_bpfAtt`, calls `_updateBPF()` - `_audioLoopSeek(t)`: checks if frame is in ring (`frame >= writeFrame - RING_SIZE && frame < writeFrame`); S.playheadTime = t; if (!_ctrl || !S.isPlaying) { scheduleRender(); return; } const frame = Math.round(t * _srcSr); const writeFrame = Atomics.load(_ctrl, 1); if (frame >= writeFrame - _RING_SIZE && frame < writeFrame) { if (_node) _node.port.postMessage({ type: 'loop_seek', frame }); if (_worker) _worker.postMessage({ type: 'loop_seek', frame }); } else { audioSeek(t); } scheduleRender(); } ``` - **`static/state.js`** — Added: ```javascript rulerLoopF0: null, // lower freq bound of ruler selection (kHz) — set on drag release rulerLoopF1: null, // upper freq bound of ruler selection (kHz) ``` - **`static/events.js`** — Multiple changes: - Added `let _bpfAttPos = null;` (canvas-space position for BPF overlay) - On real drag release: stores `rulerLoopF0 = yToF(Math.max(S.rulerY0, S.rulerY1))`, `rulerLoopF1 = yToF(Math.min(...))`, calls `audioUpdateBPF()` - On tiny drag dismiss, Escape, zoom-to-selection: clears `rulerLoopF0 = null; rulerLoopF1 = null; audioUpdateBPF()` - **`static/render.js`** — Multiple changes: - `drawRuler()`: sets `_bpfAttPos = { x: lx, y: btnY + btnH + 4, w: lw }` when `S.rulerFixed`, else `_bpfAttPos = null` - **BUG NOT YET FIXED**: `_bpfAttPos` is NOT reset to null when `drawRuler()` returns early (when `!S.isRuling && !S.rulerFixed`). Deployed. Here's what changed:

**Fix 1 — filter strength slider not disappearing:**  
`drawRuler()` now resets `_bpfAttPos = null` unconditionally at the top, before the early-return guard. Previously the early return fired when you cleared the marquee without ever touching `_bpfAttPos`, leaving the slider visible.

**Fix 2 & 3 — PSD redesign:**

- **No more zoom/drag handles.** The three helper functions (`psdFToY`, `psdYToF`, `_psdHitTest`) and all four `psdCanvas` event listeners are gone. The independent `psdViewLow`/`psdViewHigh` viewport is no longer used.

- **PSD overlays the spectrogram.** `psdCanvas` is now `position: absolute; left: 0; top: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; pointer-events: none; background: transparent` — it sits transparently on top of `mainCanvas`. The freq axis is driven by `fToY()`, so log/linear blending and the `S.freqLow`/`S.freqHigh` zoom range track the spectrogram exactly.

- **Horizontal bars, min dB at left.** Each frequency bin is plotted as a horizontal bar starting at `x = YAXIS_W` (the right edge of the freq-tick column) and extending rightward to `x = YAXIS_W + normalizedPower × specWidth`. The peak bin in the visible range always fills the full spectrogram width; quieter bins are proportionally shorter. The fill is a translucent green gradient (barely visible, 6–20% opacity) so the spectrogram remains readable underneath.

messages.text:
All three changes done:

1. **Max dB in avg mode** — `drawPSD()` was bailing early whenever `peakPow < 0.001`. In avg mode the spectrum is time-averaged, so its peak (when normalised against the global scale set by loud playhead frames) can easily fall below that threshold even when a real signal is present. Lowered the guard to `<= 0` so any non-zero signal renders the curve and label.

2. **Min conf default 75%** — `state.js` (`minConf: 0.75`) and the HTML slider (`value="75"` + `75%` span) updated together. The existing boot loop that reads `getAttribute('value')` will pick up `"75"` and call `updateTrack()` automatically, so the slider fill renders correctly on load.

3. **Legend 22 px right** — `bx` in `drawKeyboardLegend()` now starts 22 px further right (`YAXIS_W + _KL_PAD + 22 = 82 px`). "Space" was ending at x ≈ 119 px and the avg/▶ toggle occupies roughly x 56–105 px, so the word's left edge fell inside the button. At 82 + 59 = 141 px for the key column's right edge, "Space" spans ≈ 111–141 px — fully clear of the toggle.

Committed and deployed. Here's what changed:

**In the bin-collection loop**, two new trackers alongside the existing peak: `peakFreq` (kHz of the loudest visible bin), and `minPow / minY / minFreq` for the quietest visible bin.

messages.result: [main 762dd98] PSD axis labels: clamp text to canvas edges, tick stays at bin Y

messages.text:
Committed and deployed. The logic in one line:

```js
const ty = Math.max(LH, Math.min(H - LH, ly));
```

- `ly` is the true canvas Y of the bin — the tick is always drawn there.
- `ty` is the text anchor, clamped to `[LH, H−LH]` (10 px inside each edge), so the dB line above (`textBaseline:'bottom'`, −10 px) and the frequency line below (`textBaseline:'top'`, +10 px) both stay fully visible regardless of where the peak or min bin lands in the frequency range.

messages.text:
The fix has two parts:

1. **Main canvas** — pre-render all calls to a fixed-resolution offscreen canvas. `drawCallsBatched()` becomes a single `drawImage()` blit when zoomed out (viewDur > 20 s). Panning is free (just a source-rect shift); only filter/freq changes force a rebuild.

2. **Overview canvas** — currently does one `fillStyle` + `fillRect` per call (terrible for thousands of calls). Batch by species the same way the main canvas already does.

The cache uses `refPPS = min(10, floor(16384 / duration))` px/s — caps the canvas at Safari's 16 384 px hard limit for very long recordings. `contourAlpha` is applied at blit time so dragging the opacity slider never invalidates the cache.
